auditFindings.area: Coach-side nutrition plan builder (templates, per-client plans, food library, exchanges, cycles)
auditFindings.summary:
A coach builds nutrition in `/coach/nutrition-plans`, a hub with three tabs: Plantillas (templates = reusable molds), Alumnos (active per-client plans board), and Alimentos (food library). The same `PlanBuilder` client component drives both template authoring (`/new`, `/[templateId]/edit`) and per-client editing (`/client/[clientId]`), switching on a `mode: 'template' | 'client-plan'` prop. The builder is a draft-state machine: a left sidebar holds plan name, macro goals (kcal/P/C/F), an "Auto" toggle that live-syncs goals to the summed macros of the foods added, an optional Mifflin-St Jeor macro suggester (needs client weight/height from intake, plus manually-entered age/gender/activity/goal), and free-text instructions. The right canvas is a dnd-kit list of meal blocks; each meal has a name, an optional day-of-week (1-7 or "all days", Santiago TZ), a note to the client, and a list of food items. Foods are added via a portal bottom-sheet `FoodSearchDrawer` that searches the coach food library (debounced, virtualized, category-filtered, favorites-pinned), lets you pick quantity+unit (g/ml/un) with a live macro preview, or inline-create a custom food. Each food item can also carry coach-defined swap/exchange alternatives ("Configurar cambios") that the client picks from. Macros are computed purely in `lib/nutrition-utils.ts` from per-100g/ml DB values (g/ml = qty/100 factor; un = qty×serving_size/100).

Saving routes through `nutrition-coach.actions.ts`: templates go through `NutritionService.createOrUpdateTemplateFromJson` + `propagateTemplateChanges` (SYNCED plans); per-client plans go through `upsertClientNutritionPlanJson`, which snapshots the prior plan into `nutrition_plan_history`, then reconciles meals (by position in grams mode, by DB id in exchanges mode) to preserve meal ids so `nutrition_meal_logs` survive. Assigning a template to clients is `AssignModal` → `assignTemplateToClients`; editing a client's plan individually detaches it to CUSTOM. A separate paid module `nutrition_exchanges` (gated server-side per active workspace) overlays an "intercambios/porciones" mode per client-plan: instead of grams, the coach prescribes portions per exchange group (P/C/F/V/LAC/LEG...) per meal via steppers, can define day variants (Descanso/Entreno AM/PM), sees derived macros (Σ portions × group ref macros), and exports a branded PDF. There is also a per-client nutrition cycle concept (`nutrition_plan_cycles`, `upsertNutritionPlanCycle`) for periodized blocks. No Edamam/external food API is wired into the plan builder — Edamam lives only in a separate `/coach/recipes` feature; the builder's food universe is the local `foods` table (system catalog + coach custom + org foods).

strengths.title: Single builder for templates and client plans, low duplication
strengths.detail: PlanBuilder.tsx drives both modes off one `mode` prop, keeping draft state, dnd reorder, macro math, and food search identical. Reduces drift between template authoring and per-client editing.

strengths.title: Macro math is pure and centralized
strengths.detail: lib/nutrition-utils.ts (calculateFoodItemMacros) and exchange-calc.ts are IO-free and unit-testable, with a clear per-100g model and a documented fix for the liquid serving_size bug (15ml oil = 15g fat, not 210g). Same functions are reused by builder, PDF, and the client app for consistency.

strengths.title: Meal-id-preserving reconciliation protects client logs
strengths.detail: upsertClientNutritionPlanJson matches existing meals (by order_index in grams, by DB id via reconcileMealsById in exchanges) and UPDATEs in place so nutrition_meal_logs/meal_exchange_targets/day_variant survive edits — avoids silently orphaning a client's adherence history (nutrition-coach.actions.ts:334-480).

strengths.title: Auto-save history snapshot before every client-plan save
strengths.detail: Each per-client save writes a nutrition_plan_history snapshot (source 'auto_before_save') and there's a restoreClientNutritionPlanFromHistory action — real versioning/rollback for client plans.

strengths.title: Exchange (intercambios) mode is genuinely RD-shaped
strengths.detail: Portion-per-group prescription, composed groups (LEG = 1P+1C expanded), day variants, food→portion equivalences with home measures, and a 'macros referenciales' provisional badge when a group's macros aren't confirmed. This is the standard LatAm clinical-nutrition exchange-list method, not a generic macro tracker.

strengths.title: Workspace-scoped data access is consistent and careful
strengths.detail: Foods, plans, and clients are scoped 3-ways (standalone/team/enterprise) server-side with allowlist (not denylist) filtering to avoid RLS fail-open, and food search resolves org scope server-side rather than trusting the client.

strengths.title: Macro suggester grounded in client intake
strengths.detail: Sidebar offers a Mifflin-St Jeor TDEE estimate seeded from the client's real weight/height with activity/goal presets and protein g/kg guidance in tooltips — a useful starting point for the coach.

weaknesses.title: Food model only stores 4 macros — no fiber, sugar, sodium, saturated fat, or micronutrients
weaknesses.detail: The `foods` table (verified in database.types.ts) has calories/protein_g/carbs_g/fats_g/serving_size/category/brand/is_liquid only. A professional RD cannot prescribe or track fiber, sodium (hypertension/renal), sugars, saturated vs unsaturated fat, or any micronutrient (iron, calcium, etc.). This is a hard ceiling on clinical nutrition; the app is a macro tool, not a dietetics tool.

weaknesses.title: No micronutrient/fiber means goals are macro-only too
weaknesses.detail: Sidebar goals are kcal/P/C/F exclusively. There's no way to set or display a fiber target, sodium limit, or hydration goal, which are routine in real meal plans. Combined with the food model gap, the whole nutrition surface is macro-locked.

weaknesses.title: Per-food data quality is unverified and coach-entered
weaknesses.detail: Custom foods are free-entry (FoodSearchDrawer create + AddFoodSheet) with no source, no validation against a reference DB, and macros rounded to integers on create (Math.round in addCoachCustomFood/saveCustomFood). No Edamam/USDA/national food table integration in the builder, so accuracy depends entirely on what the coach types, and rounding 'per 100g' to whole numbers loses precision for low-calorie or high-fat items.

weaknesses.title: Templates cannot use exchange mode — exchanges are client-plan-only
weaknesses.detail: exchangeEnabled is gated on `mode === 'client-plan'` (PlanBuilder.tsx:99). A coach who works primarily in exchange lists cannot build a reusable exchange-based template; they must redo portions per client. The two systems (template propagation vs exchanges) don't compose.

weaknesses.title: Exchange targets persist on a per-keystroke debounce, decoupled from the Save button
weaknesses.detail: handleExchangeTargetsChange auto-saves portions via saveMealExchangeTargetsAction (700ms debounce) independent of the main Save, while grams data only persists on Save. This split-brain persistence model is confusing: in exchange mode some edits are live-saved and others (meal name, instructions, goals) are not, with no unified dirty/saved indicator for the plan as a whole.

weaknesses.title: Macro reconciliation between exchange portions and gram goals is manual and can silently disagree
weaknesses.detail: In exchange mode autoSync is forced off and goals become the RD's requirement, while derived totals come from Σ portions. The panel shows derived/goal side by side but nothing enforces or warns hard when they diverge beyond a threshold (the 5% mismatch banner is grams-mode only, sidebar). A coach can ship a plan whose prescribed portions don't meet the stated kcal/protein goal.

weaknesses.title: Day-of-week scheduling is coarse; no week/cycle view in the builder
weaknesses.detail: Each meal carries a single day_of_week (or all-days). There's no notion of a full weekly structure, training vs rest day templating beyond exchange 'variants', or a calendar preview. Cycles exist as a separate data model (nutrition_plan_cycles) but are not surfaced in this builder UI, so periodization is disconnected from plan authoring.

weaknesses.title: Grams-mode meal reconciliation keys on order_index, which is fragile
weaknesses.detail: Non-exchange saves match existing meals by position (existingByIndex on order_index). Reordering meals in grams mode reassigns logs by slot rather than identity — the exchanges path was explicitly fixed to match by id to avoid exactly this corruption, but the grams path still relies on positional matching, so a reorder+save can attach a client's historical meal logs to a different meal.

weaknesses.title: No portion/recipe reuse inside the builder
weaknesses.detail: saved_meals/template_meal_groups exist in the schema and queries (getCoachSavedMeals) but the PlanBuilder food flow only adds individual foods; there's no 'insert saved meal/recipe' affordance in the canvas, so coaches rebuild common meals food-by-food every time.

weaknesses.title: Swap options duplicate full food snapshots into JSON, risking staleness
weaknesses.detail: Each swap option stores a denormalized copy of the alternative food's macros in food_items.swap_options (PlanBuilder handleSave). If the underlying food is later corrected, existing plans keep the old snapshot with no resync path, diverging from the live foods table.

auditFindings.fragmentation: Nutrition authoring is spread across several partly-overlapping surfaces. (1) Two food-creation paths with different code: the inline create inside FoodSearchDrawer (addCoachCustomFood, JSON action) and the standalone AddFoodSheet (saveCustomFood, FormData legacy action) — duplicated validation/rounding logic. (2) Two food-browse UIs: FoodSearchDrawer (in-builder) and FoodBrowser/FoodListCompact (foods tab), both calling searchCoachFoodLibrary but with separate category normalization. (3) Persistence is split: grams data saved atomically on Save, exchange portions/variants/mode auto-saved via separate debounced actions (exchange.actions.ts), and cycles via yet another action — no single source of truth for 'is this plan dirty/saved'. (4) Templates (nutrition_plan_templates + template_meals + template_meal_groups + saved_meals + saved_meal_items) and client plans (nutrition_plans + nutrition_meals + food_items) are two parallel schemas the builder flattens into the same MealDraft shape, with propagateTemplateChanges bridging them; exchanges add a third overlay (meal_exchange_targets, day_variants) that only attaches to client plans. (5) Legacy FormData server actions (saveNutritionTemplate, parseLegacyTemplateMeals, saveCustomFood) coexist with the JSON actions, so two serialization formats are live. (6) Recipes (/coach/recipes, Edamam-backed) are a completely separate nutrition surface not integrated into the plan builder at all.
auditFindings.dataFlow: Tables touched: foods (catalog + coach/org custom, 4-macro only), nutrition_plan_templates / template_meals / template_meal_groups / saved_meals / saved_meal_items (template side), nutrition_plans / nutrition_meals / food_items (client side), nutrition_plan_history (auto snapshots), nutrition_plan_cycles (periodization), meal_exchange_targets / day_variants / exchange_groups / exchange_food_equivalences (intercambios module), daily_nutrition_logs / nutrition_meal_logs (adherence, read for the board and preserved across edits), client_food_preferences (favorites pin in search). Flow: RSC page (_data/*.queries.ts via React.cache) → PlanBuilder draft state → server actions (_actions/nutrition-coach.actions.ts, exchange.actions.ts, food-library.actions.ts) → NutritionService (services/nutrition.service.ts) and direct supabase calls → revalidatePath. Macro computation is client+server shared via lib/nutrition-utils.ts and services/nutrition-exchanges/exchange-calc.ts. Note the data flow partly bypasses the documented repository layer: actions call supabase directly (food_items/nutrition_meals inserts) rather than always going through infrastructure/db/nutrition.repository.ts.
auditFindings.professionalGaps: For a registered dietitian/nutritionist the builder is a competent macro-and-exchange tool but misses clinical essentials: (1) No fiber, sodium, sugar, saturated/unsaturated fat, or any micronutrient — impossible to manage hypertension, renal, diabetes, pregnancy, or deficiency cases. (2) No food data provenance or verified reference database in the builder (no USDA/INTA/national table, no barcode); macros are coach-typed and integer-rounded, so accuracy is unreliable. (3) No per-meal timing beyond a free-text note (no breakfast/lunch structure, no pre/post-workout scheduling primitives). (4) No hydration, supplements, or meal-prep/recipe instructions as structured fields. (5) Macro goals aren't reconciled against the actual prescription in exchange mode (no hard validation that portions meet the kcal/protein target), and there's no per-kg or %-of-kcal target view. (6) No allergy/intolerance/dislike enforcement — client_food_preferences only pins favorites in search, it doesn't block prescribing an allergen. (7) Periodization (cycles) is a separate, non-integrated model, so progressive plans must be managed outside the main authoring flow. (8) No client-facing rationale, no export of a clinical-format plan (only a compact/equivalences PDF in exchange mode; grams mode has no documented PDF here). Net: good for a personal-training macro coach, insufficient for licensed clinical dietetics.

auditFindings.area: Coach view of a student: nutrition surfaces (client-detail tabs + analytics + dashboard KPIs/sheets)
auditFindings.summary:
On the coach's single-client page (`/coach/clients/[clientId]`), the entire profile is one giant client component `ClientProfileDashboard` driven by a single `data` blob (typed `any`) coming from `client-detail.service.ts::getClientDetailData`. It renders a 6-tab nav (`ProfileTabNav`: Overview, Progreso, Análisis, Plan, Nutrición, Facturación). Nutrition is NOT confined to the Nutrición tab — it bleeds into at least 4 of the 6 tabs. The dedicated "Nutrición" tab renders `NutritionTabB5` (1411 lines, ~12 distinct GlassCards: coach alerts panel, check-in×nutrition cross card, diet-cycle/history section, favorite foods, active-plan macros + pie, today macro rings, 30d adherence heatmap/strip + monthly/streak/week-vs-prev stats, daily habits, 7d kcal chart, objective-vs-adherence composed chart, today-consumed pie, full meal list, last-logged-day, 30-row log table, and a per-date day navigator with read-only day view). Separately, the OVERVIEW tab shows a third nutrition adherence ring (`ProfileOverviewB3` "Nutrición (7d)") plus a "Nutri. en riesgo" red dot inside `ProfileProgramSummaryCard` (a program card), plus a nutrition line in the top alert banner (`getProfileTopAlert`). The PROGRESO tab embeds 3 more nutrition charts inside the shared "Panel de Progreso Unificado" pill toggle (Macros, Adherencia calórica, Balance neto) — nutrition analytics living under a Progress/body-composition panel, not under Nutrición. The Nutrición TAB BADGE itself shows either a risk "!" or a meal-detail count.

On the multi-client DASHBOARD (`/coach/dashboard`), nutrition appears as a sub-hint on the single "Adherencia" KPI (`KpiStrip`: `Nutricion: {avgNutrition}%`) and as a second tab inside `ClientStatsSheet` (Adherencia | Nutrición), both fed by `dashboard.service.ts::getDirectoryPulse` → `mapDirectoryPulseToNutritionStats`. So a coach must cross three different pages/surfaces (dashboard KPI hint, dashboard sheet tab, client Overview ring, client Nutrición tab, client Progreso charts) to see "nutrition" for one student, and the adherence number is computed by at least two different, non-equivalent formulas.

auditFindings.fragmentation:
Nutrition is scattered across at least 9 distinct visual entry points spanning 2 pages, with NO single canonical 'nutrition for this client' view:

DASHBOARD (multi-client):
1. KpiStrip — nutrition is a tiny secondary hint string ('Nutricion: {avgNutrition}%') glued under the 'Adherencia' KPI; not its own KPI.
2. ClientStatsSheet — nutrition is the 2nd tab behind Adherencia; easy to miss.

CLIENT DETAIL (single client) — nutrition appears in 4 of 6 tabs:
3. ProfileTabNav badge — 'Nutrición' tab shows '!' (risk) OR a meal count; the same tab badge overloads two different meanings.
4. OVERVIEW tab → ProfileOverviewB3 'Nutrición (7d)' ring — a THIRD adherence ring, duplicating the weekly number that also appears inside the Nutrición tab's adherence strip stats.
5. OVERVIEW tab → ProfileProgramSummaryCard — a red 'Nutri. en riesgo' dot living inside the *program/training* card (nutrition status attached to a non-nutrition card).
6. OVERVIEW tab → ProfileTopAlertBanner via getProfileTopAlert — a nutrition<60% message, but it COMPETES with check-in/workout/plan rules in a priority cascade, so the nutrition alert is silently suppressed whenever a workout-gap or stale-checkin rule fires first.
7. PROGRESO tab → 'Panel de Progreso Unificado' pill toggle hides 3 nutrition charts (Macros / Adherencia calórica / Balance Neto) UNDER a progress/body-composition panel — nutrition analytics filed away from the Nutrición tab entirely.
8. NUTRICIÓN tab → NutritionTabB5 — ~12 stacked cards (alerts, checkin-cross, cycle+history, favorites, plan macros, today rings, 30d heatmap/strip + monthly/streak/week-delta, habits, 7d kcal, objective-vs-adherence, consumed pie, full meal list, last day, 30-row table, per-date viewer). It mixes plan EDITING (cycle editor, history restore, duplicate-to-client, links to /coach/nutrition-plans/client) with read-only ANALYTICS in one endless scroll.
9. NUTRICIÓN tab also links OUT to two other places ('Editar plan' → /coach/nutrition-plans/client/[id], 'Ver como alumno' → /c/[slug]/nutrition), so the actual plan builder is yet another surface.

DUPLICATION / INCONSISTENT DATA (the worst part):
- Adherence is computed by TWO non-equivalent formulas. client-detail.service `avgMealCompliance` / monthly loop counts done/total over ALL nutrition_meal_logs of a day with NO day-of-week applicability filter, whereas dashboard.service `getDirectoryPulse` AND the client's own today `nutritionCompliancePercent` DO filter meals via `nutritionMealAppliesOnIsoYmdInSantiago`. Result: the weekly/monthly % shown in the Overview ring and the Nutrición-tab strip can legitimately differ from the 'today' % and from the dashboard number for the same student — coaches see conflicting adherence figures depending on where they look.
- 'Adherence' itself is overloaded: the heatmap legend says green = 'completó al menos una comida' (boolean log existence), the AdherenceStrip uses plan-meal %, the ring uses weekly avg %, and the badge uses today's %. Four definitions of the same word on adjacent cards (the component even ships InfoTooltips apologizing for this).
- ClientProfileDashboard RE-DERIVES nutritionHistory, consumed macros, diferencial/acumulado INLINE (lines 170-216) from raw logs, partially duplicating the service's `nutritionLogsEnriched` (it prefers `nutritionLogsEnriched` but falls back to recomputing), so two code paths can produce different consumed_calories.
- Weekly nutrition % is passed redundantly into NutritionTabB5, ProfileOverviewB3, NutritionCheckinContextCard, and getProfileTopAlert from `compliance.nutritionWeeklyAvgPct` — fine, but each card re-labels/re-thresholds it (70/50 ring colors vs 60 risk vs 38 caution vs 30/45 alert thresholds) with no shared constant, so 'at risk' means a different cutoff on every card.

NAVIGATION CONFUSION:
- A coach wanting 'how is this client's nutrition' has no one button; they must: open the client, scan Overview ring + program red-dot + maybe-suppressed top alert, then switch to Nutrición tab and scroll past ~12 cards, then ALSO check Progreso tab's hidden pill charts, then click out to the separate plan builder. The Nutrición tab and Progreso tab both contain nutrition charts with overlapping data but different framings.
- The tab is labelled 'Nutrición' but the actual plan authoring lives on a different route (/coach/nutrition-plans/...), so the tab is half-dashboard half-launchpad.
auditFindings.dataFlow: Two independent server entry points feed nutrition. (1) Single client: `client-detail.service.ts::getClientDetailData(clientId)` queries `nutrition_plans`, `daily_nutrition_logs` (40-day window) with nested `nutrition_meal_logs`→`nutrition_meals`, today's `daily_nutrition_logs`, active plan full with `nutrition_meals`+food_items, `nutrition_plan_cycles`, `nutrition_plan_templates`, `nutrition_plan_history`, plus client favorite foods. It returns ~20 `nutrition*`-prefixed fields (nutritionLogs, nutritionLogsEnriched, activeNutritionPlanWithMeals, nutritionAdherence30d, nutritionMonthlyAvgPct, nutritionStreakDays, todayConsumedMacros, hasTodayNutritionLog, nutritionPlanCycles/TemplatesLite/HistoryEntries, mealDetails) AND a `compliance` object with nutritionCompliancePercent/WeeklyAvgPct/PrevWeeklyAvgPct. (2) Multi-client: `dashboard.service.ts::getDirectoryPulse(coachId)` chunk-queries `daily_nutrition_logs`+`nutrition_meal_logs`+`nutrition_meals` for all clients, computing `nutritionPercentage` per client WITH day-of-week applicability filtering (nutritionMealAppliesOnIsoYmdInSantiago), then `mapDirectoryPulseToNutritionStats` + avgNutrition. The client page additionally recomputes nutrition analytics client-side in ClientProfileDashboard. Tables touched: nutrition_plans, daily_nutrition_logs, nutrition_meal_logs, nutrition_meals, nutrition_plan_cycles, nutrition_plan_templates, nutrition_plan_history. Server actions in `_actions/client-detail.actions.ts` (getClientNutritionForDate / ActivityDates / HabitsForDate) and `nutrition-coach.actions.ts` (upsert cycle, restore history, duplicate plan) are called lazily from the client tab.

strengths.title: Deterministic, no-IA coach alerts are well isolated
strengths.detail: nutrition-coach-alerts.ts and nutrition-checkin-coach-copy.ts are pure functions with clear thresholds and good explanatory copy, reused via props with no extra queries — the right pattern; they're just under-used (only in the Nutrición tab).

strengths.title: Rich per-day forensic detail exists
strengths.detail: NutritionTabB5's per-date DayNavigator + NutritionDayReadOnly shows completed meals, food items, and applied food swaps — genuinely useful auditing a nutritionist would value, and consumed macros use a shared calculateConsumedMacrosWithCompletionFallback + portion map.

strengths.title: Plan history/autosave + cycle phasing is real functionality
strengths.detail: NutritionCycleHistorySection gives week-based diet phases with automatic current-block resolution and snapshot restore that preserves adherence logs — solid coaching tooling.

strengths.title: Service does parallelize queries
strengths.detail: getClientDetailData uses Promise.all and a narrowed nutritionPromise select('id,is_active'); dashboard chunks client ids — performance-conscious despite the data sprawl.

weaknesses.title: Two divergent adherence formulas produce conflicting numbers for the same client
weaknesses.detail: client-detail.service avgMealCompliance/monthly count done/total over ALL meal logs of a day with NO day-of-week applicability filter; dashboard.service.getDirectoryPulse and the client's own 'today' percent DO filter via nutritionMealAppliesOnIsoYmdInSantiago. The Overview ring / Nutrición strip (weekly/monthly) can therefore disagree with the dashboard KPI and the 'today' badge for the same student. There is no single source of truth for 'nutrition adherence'.

weaknesses.title: 'Adherence' has 4 incompatible definitions shown on adjacent cards
weaknesses.detail: heatmap legend = 'logged at least one meal' (boolean), AdherenceStrip = % of plan meals, ring = weekly avg %, badge/risk = today %. The UI even ships apologetic InfoTooltips. A coach cannot trust what a single % means.

weaknesses.title: Nutrition scattered across 4 of 6 tabs with no canonical view
weaknesses.detail: Overview ring + program-card risk dot + top-alert + Nutrición tab (12 cards) + Progreso-tab hidden pill charts. Nutrition analytics (Macros/Adherencia/Balance) are filed under the PROGRESO/body-composition panel, not the Nutrición tab — actively misleading navigation.

weaknesses.title: Nutrition top-alert silently suppressed by priority cascade
weaknesses.detail: getProfileTopAlert returns only ONE alert; the nutrition<60% rule sits below stale-checkin and workout-gap rules, so for a client who also missed workouts the nutrition warning never surfaces on Overview — coach may never see it unless they open the Nutrición tab.

weaknesses.title: NutritionTabB5 is a 1411-line client component mixing edit + analytics + 12 cards
weaknesses.detail: Single endless-scroll component combining plan editing (cycle editor, history restore, duplicate-to-client, external links) with read-only analytics, heavy charts (recharts) and per-date lazy fetches. Hard to maintain, no sub-componentization of the cards, uses many `any`/`unknown` casts.

weaknesses.title: Inline re-derivation of nutrition data in the page component duplicates the service
weaknesses.detail: ClientProfileDashboard recomputes nutritionHistory/consumed macros/diferencial/acumulado (lines 170-216) from raw logs even though the service already produces nutritionLogsEnriched, with a fallback that can diverge. Logic lives in two layers.

weaknesses.title: Per-card threshold constants are hard-coded and inconsistent
weaknesses.detail: Ring colors at 70/50, risk at 60, caution at 38, alerts at 30/45/1200kcal, streak at 80% — no shared config. 'At risk' means a different cutoff on every surface, so badges/dots/rings can disagree for one client.

weaknesses.title: Entire client `data` typed as `any`
weaknesses.detail: ClientProfileDashboard takes `data: any` ('to save time'); ~20 nutrition fields flow untyped into props, so renaming/missing fields fail silently at runtime instead of compile time.

weaknesses.title: Nutrition under-represented on the dashboard
weaknesses.detail: On the multi-client dashboard nutrition is only a hint string under one KPI and a hidden 2nd sheet tab; a coach scanning the roster cannot triage who is failing nutrition without drilling into each client.

auditFindings.professionalGaps: A registered dietitian (RD) would find the coach view limited: (1) Adherence is measured purely as 'meals checked off', never against actual intake — there is no comparison of consumed vs target protein/carbs/fat distribution over time at a glance per client (macros exist only as today rings / stacked daily bars, no rolling target-hit %). (2) No micronutrient/fiber tracking; the only safety rail is a hard-coded 'kcal<1200' danger alert. (3) Consumed macros are estimated from 'meal marked done × portion' (calculateConsumedMacrosWithCompletionFallback), not from what the client actually logged eating, so the RD cannot trust the consumed numbers for clinical decisions. (4) No way to set/track non-weight nutrition goals (e.g., protein floor g/kg, hydration target vs body mass) — habits (water/steps/sleep/fasting/supplements) are shown raw with arbitrary color thresholds, not against any prescription. (5) Weight×adherence cross-card is a single heuristic caution string, not a trend/rate-of-change analysis an RD would use to judge deficit sustainability. (6) No export/print of a nutrition summary for the client record. (7) Fragmentation itself is a professional gap: an RD doing a weekly review must hop between Overview, Nutrición, Progreso tabs and the separate plan builder, with adherence numbers that don't reconcile — undermining trust in the data.

auditFindings.area: Alumno/client-side nutrition experience (white-label PWA): /c/[coach_slug]/nutrition + dashboard nutrition summary
auditFindings.summary:
The student nutrition flow is a coach-prescribed (not self-logged) model: the alumno does NOT search a food database or enter grams. They receive an active plan (one per client, `nutrition_plans` with `is_active=true`) whose meals + food_items + macros are authored by the coach. The student's job is adherence tracking — they tap to mark each meal complete, and macro rings/bars fill toward coach-defined daily targets via a "completion fallback" (completing a meal credits its full planned macros). Beyond binary completion there are three refinements: a partial-portion picker (25/50/75/100% of the plan, scaling credited macros), an emoji satisfaction score (1-3, surfaced to the coach), and a food-swap mechanism limited to alternatives the coach pre-configured per ingredient (the student only picks, the coach's portion is applied). An optional exchanges module (porciones/intercambios) overlays code chips ("2C · 1LAC") and a bottom-sheet equivalences table when enabled. The page also hosts a separate HabitsTracker (water/steps/sleep/fasting/supplements), a 30-day adherence heatmap strip, a streak banner, a confetti delight on day-completion, and rich WhatsApp/PDF export.

The architecture is solid and offline-aware: a localStorage read-model cache lets the plan + today's log render without network, meal toggles queue offline and drain on reconnect (OfflineNutritionQueueSync, mounted app-wide), and the no-plan path falls back to a cached copy. The dashboard surfaces a condensed NutritionDailySummary (calorie bar + 3 MacroBars + per-meal toggle rows + compliance ring) duplicating much of the page logic. Server actions are correctly auth-guarded (every action re-checks `auth.getUser()` and `user.id === clientId`) and swaps are validated against the coach's allowed options server-side.

auditFindings.dataFlow: Tables: nutrition_plans (active per client) → nutrition_meals → food_items → foods (coach-authored); daily_nutrition_logs (one per client/plan/day, UNIQUE client_id,plan_id,log_date) → nutrition_meal_logs (is_completed, consumed_quantity=partial pct, satisfaction_score) + nutrition_meal_food_swaps (client-chosen swap of coach options); daily_habits (water/steps/sleep/fasting/supplements/notes); client_food_preferences (favorite/dislike). Reads via _data/*.queries.ts (React.cache). Writes via 'use server' actions calling supabase RLS-scoped client; revalidatePath on /nutrition + /dashboard. Macros are NEVER stored consumed — they are computed client+server from plan macros × completion/portion via calculateConsumedMacrosWithCompletionFallback in lib/nutrition-utils. target_*_at_log columns snapshot the plan goals at log creation.
auditFindings.fragmentation: Macro computation and the swap-application transform are duplicated nearly verbatim in three places: NutritionShell (mealsVisibleWithSwaps, ~55 lines), NutritionDailySummary dashboard (mealsWithSwaps, ~45 lines), and partially in the copy/PDF builders inside the shell. The swap-merge map-building logic is copy-pasted between the page shell and the dashboard summary with identical shapes — any change to swap semantics must be made in 2+ files. Macro color coding is inconsistent across surfaces: MealCard/MealIngredientRow/MacroRingSummary use orange(P)/blue(C)/yellow(G), but the dashboard MacroBar uses rose(P)/amber(C)/emerald(G) — same data, different colors on the same user's two screens. 'Adherence' is also defined inconsistently: AdherenceStrip legend/counter counts a day as registered if completed>0 (≥1 meal), the heatmap cell color buckets by completed/planned ratio, but the streak counts a day only at ≥50% of applicable meals, and the dashboard ComplianceRing nutritionScore uses yet another server-side score — four different 'adherence' definitions the student sees simultaneously. Meal toggle logic + offline-enqueue is duplicated between NutritionShell.handleToggle and dashboard MealCompletionRow.
auditFindings.professionalGaps: A registered dietitian would find the model fundamentally adherence-tracking, not intake-tracking: the student cannot log anything the coach didn't pre-plan. There is no free food search, no barcode scan, no 'I ate something off-plan' entry, no water/meal photo, no portion entry in real grams — only 25/50/75/100% of the planned portion, which cannot represent eating MORE than planned or a substitution outside the coach's pre-set swap list. Consequently 'consumed' macros are an estimate of plan-adherence, not actual dietary intake, so the macro rings can read 100% while the client ate something entirely different. No fiber, sugar, sodium, saturated fat, or micronutrients are surfaced (only kcal/P/C/F). Hydration target is unlabeled (water chips have no goal/comparison). No meal-timing or per-meal target windows. Satisfaction is a 3-point emoji, not hunger/fullness or symptom logging. Habits explicitly disclaim clinical value but there's no allergy/intolerance, GI, mood, or adherence-barrier capture. Streak/adherence reward marking meals done regardless of whether the food was actually eaten — gameable, and could reinforce dishonest self-report. No weight/measurement linkage shown on this surface to contextualize adherence vs outcome.

strengths.title: Very low logging friction (1 tap per meal)
strengths.detail: Marking a meal complete is a single tap on the 44px checkbox (haptic vibrate(50)), with optimistic UI (useOptimistic) so it feels instant. Completing the last meal of the day fires confetti once/date. This beats consumer apps like MyFitnessPal for the prescribed-plan use case where the student just confirms adherence rather than searching/weighing foods. Day-completion, portion adjust, and satisfaction are all progressive-disclosure inside the expanded card so the default path stays one tap.

strengths.title: Genuinely robust offline/PWA support
strengths.detail: localStorage read-model cache renders plan + today's log offline; meal toggles enqueue (dedup by mealId+date, last-write-wins) and auto-drain on 'online' via an app-wide OfflineNutritionQueueSync; the no-plan path recovers from cache; clear amber offline banners. This is unusually thorough for a web PWA and directly serves gym clients on flaky mobile data.

strengths.title: Thoughtful motivation/clarity layer
strengths.detail: 30-day adherence heatmap, streak banner with tiered copy, macro rings with over-target warning state (red AlertTriangle), confetti, and pervasive InfoTooltips explaining how streak/adherence/macros are computed. Read-only historical days are clearly locked with explanation. Accessibility is strong: ARIA labels on rings/progressbar/heatmap cells, reduced-motion respected throughout.

strengths.title: Coach-bounded food swaps with server validation
strengths.detail: Swaps are constrained to coach-pre-configured alternatives per ingredient, validated server-side against food_items.swap_options, with the coach's portion applied and a live macro preview. This keeps the plan clinically controlled while giving the student flexibility — a good B2B2C compromise.

strengths.title: Strong white-label + export polish
strengths.detail: Brand resolved server-side from proxy headers (free tier => EVA), PDF export with tenant brand and a separate exchanges-pauta PDF, plus two WhatsApp copy formats (detail/short). Good for coach-client communication outside the app.

weaknesses.title: Four conflicting definitions of 'adherence' shown at once
weaknesses.detail: On the same screen: AdherenceStrip counter says 'X/30 días' counting any day with ≥1 completed meal; the heatmap cell color buckets by completed/planned ratio (green ≥80%, amber ≥50%, red 1-49%); the streak counts a day only at ≥50% of applicable meals; and the dashboard ComplianceRing nutritionScore is a different server score. A student sees a green-ish streak, a different heatmap color, and a different ring % for the same behavior — confusing and erodes trust in the numbers.

weaknesses.title: Macro 'consumed' is plan-adherence, not real intake
weaknesses.detail: calculateConsumedMacrosWithCompletionFallback credits a completed meal's FULL planned macros (or 25/50/75% of plan). There is no way to log eating off-plan or eating more than 100% of a meal. The macro rings can read at goal while actual intake differs entirely. For any client/coach treating these numbers as real macro tracking (which the UI strongly implies with kcal/P/C/F rings), this is misleading. At minimum the UI should label these as 'plan adherence' rather than consumed intake.

weaknesses.title: Per-call auth.getUser() network round-trip on every action
weaknesses.detail: Every server action (toggle, portion, satisfaction, swap, favorites, habits) calls supabase.auth.getUser(), which per the project's own memory hits the Auth server (not local getClaims). With the one-tap-per-meal pattern plus dashboard duplicate plus offline drain replaying the queue item-by-item, this multiplies auth calls — relevant given the documented Supabase Micro auth burst limits (1800/h per IP). Consider getClaims for these high-frequency writes.

weaknesses.title: Duplicated swap/macro transform across shell and dashboard
weaknesses.detail: The ~45-55 line swap-merge + normalizeMealForMacros pipeline is copy-pasted between NutritionShell and NutritionDailySummary; meal-toggle+offline-enqueue is duplicated between NutritionShell.handleToggle and MealCompletionRow. Divergence risk: a swap-semantics fix in one surface silently leaves the other wrong. Extract a shared hook/util.

weaknesses.title: Inconsistent macro colors between page and dashboard
weaknesses.detail: P/C/F are orange/blue/yellow on the nutrition page (MacroRingSummary, MealCard, MealIngredientRow) but rose/amber/emerald on the dashboard MacroBar. Same user, same macros, two color languages — undermines the visual consistency a polished consumer app maintains.

weaknesses.title: Refetch-after-write doubles round-trips and can clobber optimistic state
weaknesses.detail: After nearly every successful action the shell does setOptimistic AND awaits fetchLogForDate to re-pull the whole day log, then setCurrentLog. This is an extra query per tap on top of revalidatePath, and on slow networks the optimistic value is held until the refetch resolves, making fast multi-meal logging feel laggy. The optimistic state + revalidation should suffice without an explicit refetch in the happy path.

weaknesses.title: No real intake / off-plan / hydration-goal capture limits nutritionist value
weaknesses.detail: No free-food entry, no fiber/sugar/sodium/micros, water chips have no target, no hunger/fullness or symptom/allergy logging. The streak rewards marking meals done regardless of what was actually eaten (gameable). For a professional nutrition product these gaps cap how much a coach can actually adjust based on the data.

weaknesses.title: Partial-portion and satisfaction are buried behind two interactions
weaknesses.detail: To set portion % or satisfaction the student must (1) complete the meal, then (2) expand the card. Discoverability is low; many students will never find the 25/50/75% nuance, so the macro data quality the feature promises is rarely realized. Acceptable as progressive disclosure but worth a one-time hint.

auditFindings.area: Org/Enterprise & Teams nutrition (templates, sharing, scoping, RLS)
auditFindings.summary:
EVA's org-level nutrition is built around org admins (org_owner/org_admin) publishing reusable nutrition templates that enterprise coaches consume when building client plans. There are TWO parallel, disconnected org-template systems. (1) `org_nutrition_templates` is a standalone table (its own RLS, meal_names jsonb of {name,order_index,description}) surfaced on /org/[slug]/nutrition. It is purely informational: admins create/delete it, coaches can SELECT it via RLS, but NO code path actually consumes it into a real nutrition_plan — the page's "usage" is a fragile heuristic that scans active nutrition_plans and matches by EXACT name + 4 macro values. (2) `nutrition_plan_templates` rows with coach_id=NULL + org_id set (the "Templates plan completos" section + /org/[slug]/nutrition/new) ARE assignable: `assignOrgNutritionPlanTemplateToClientsAction` bulk-creates nutrition_plans for every active org client that already has a coach, copying only macros (no meals), deactivating prior active plans. All writes go through the service-role client after `getOrgAdminContext` gates on slug+admin role; reads go through the user-scoped client relying on RLS (`is_org_admin_member` lets admins see all org nutrition_plans; coaches get a SELECT policy on coach_id=NULL+org_id templates).

Scoping is org_id-centric for the enterprise form and coach_id+org_id for materialized plans. Teams are a completely separate codepath: there is NO team nutrition UI under /t/[team_slug], and team template sharing lives entirely in a different table column — `nutrition_plan_templates.team_id` (added in the F7 exchanges migration) is a flat pool-sharing marker (coach_id = author ALWAYS, team_id = scope marker, guarded by RESTRICTIVE tenant policies). org_nutrition_templates has no team_id and teams never use it. So org nutrition and team nutrition share zero code.

strengths.title: Writes consistently go through service-role behind an admin gate
strengths.detail: Every mutating action (create/delete org_nutrition_templates, create/assign nutrition_plan_templates) calls resolveAdminContext -> getOrgAdminContext(supabase, user.id, orgSlug) which validates the caller is an active org_owner/org_admin of THAT slug before using createServiceRoleClient. org_id is always taken from ctx.org.id, never from the request body, matching the project's 'never trust org_id from body' rule. Every mutation also writes an org audit event.

strengths.title: RLS is layered and defense-in-depth for the enterprise path
strengths.detail: org_nutrition_templates has admin-manage + coach-read + service_role policies. nutrition_plans/nutrition_plan_templates use SECURITY DEFINER helpers (is_org_admin_member / is_org_coach_member / is_org_coach_assigned_to_client) so the org dashboard reads (which use the user-scoped client) are RLS-enforced, not relying solely on app filters. The assignable-template read policy correctly pins coach_id IS NULL AND org_id IS NOT NULL.

strengths.title: Assignment is multi-tenant-careful
strengths.detail: assignOrgNutritionPlanTemplateToClientsAction re-fetches the template with .eq('org_id', ctx.org.id).is('coach_id', null) (can't assign another org's or a coach-owned template), scopes the deactivate-then-insert to org_id + the org's own client ids, and skips clients without a coach (coach_id is required on nutrition_plans).

weaknesses.title: Two competing org template tables with no bridge — org_nutrition_templates is essentially a dead-end
weaknesses.detail: org_nutrition_templates (rich: meal_names, instructions, goal/macros) is created and displayed but NO coach-facing flow ever reads it to build a plan — there is no foreign key from nutrition_plans to it and no assign action. The only assignable org templates live in nutrition_plan_templates (coach_id=NULL), which carry NO meals at all (assignment copies macros only, instructions=description). So the meal structure admins carefully define in org_nutrition_templates.meal_names is never propagated anywhere. Two tables, two forms (CreateOrgNutritionTemplateForm modal vs /nutrition/new page), overlapping fields, divergent semantics, on the same page.

weaknesses.title: Template 'usage' analytics are a brittle name+macro string match, not a real link
weaknesses.detail: findOrgNutritionTemplateUsage joins org_nutrition_templates to nutrition_plans by EXACT equality of name AND all four macro integers (template.name === plan.name && daily_calories/protein/carbs/fats all equal). The page even labels it 'MVP sin migracion ... match por nombre + macros'. Any coach rename, a 1-kcal tweak, or two templates with identical macros silently breaks/cross-attributes adherence, alumno counts and per-coach stats shown to the org owner. nutrition_plan_templates HAS a real template_id FK on nutrition_plans (set by the assign action) that this analytic ignores.

weaknesses.title: Teams have no nutrition template management surface at all
weaknesses.detail: There is no /t/[team_slug]/**/nutrition route and no team equivalent of the org nutrition page. Team nutrition sharing exists only at the DB layer via nutrition_plan_templates.team_id (a flat pool marker added in the F7 exchanges migration, shared by author). For the 'third form' of EVA, org nutrition is first-class (dedicated dashboard) while team nutrition is bolted-on / DB-only with no admin tooling — asymmetric with how the prompt frames teams as a permanent feature.

weaknesses.title: Bulk assign is destructive and non-selective despite UI implying a subset
weaknesses.detail: The action's own docstring says 'or a subset' but the implementation always targets ALL active org clients with a coach; AssignOrgNutritionTemplateButton offers no per-client selection. It unconditionally deactivates every prior active nutrition_plan for those clients (is_active=false) and inserts new macro-only plans, wiping any meals/customization a coach had built, with only a confirm-less modal warning. No transaction wraps the deactivate+insert, so a partial failure can leave clients with zero active plan.

weaknesses.title: org_id scoping on nutrition tables is nullable and weakly enforced at write-time for the standalone path
weaknesses.detail: nutrition_plans.org_id / nutrition_plan_templates.org_id are nullable with the standalone branch (org_id IS NULL AND coach_id = auth.uid()). The chk_nutrition_template_owner only guarantees coach_id OR org_id is set, not that org_id matches the coach's actual org. An org coach creating a plan could in principle persist org_id NULL (standalone-looking) and escape the org dashboard's org_id-filtered queries, since the dashboard reads rely on np.org_id being populated (backfilled once from clients, not trigger-maintained going forward).

weaknesses.title: foods/saved_meals/food_swap_groups org_id added but inert; org-scoped food catalogs not realized
weaknesses.detail: Migration 20260608130000 adds org_id to the catalog tables but ships no RLS or query consumption ('inert until step B'). Nothing in the org nutrition code reads an org food catalog, so org templates/plans still draw from the global/standalone foods catalog — a dual coach's standalone foods leak into org context as the migration comment itself flags.

auditFindings.dataFlow: Tables: org_nutrition_templates (org_id, meal_names jsonb, created_by) — standalone, informational. nutrition_plan_templates (coach_id nullable, org_id, team_id) — coach_id=NULL+org_id = assignable org template; team_id = flat team pool marker (separate F7 system). nutrition_plans (coach_id, client_id, org_id, template_id, is_active, is_custom) — materialized client plans. daily_nutrition_logs (plan_id, client_id, log_date) — adherence source. Services/queries: getOrgAdminContext gate -> service-role writes; getOrgNutritionTemplates/getOrgNutritionTemplateUsage/getOrgNutritionPlanTemplates/getOrgActiveNutritionPlans (React.cache, user-scoped client, RLS via is_org_admin_member/is_org_coach_member). Assign flow: nutrition_plan_templates(coach_id NULL) -> bulk INSERT nutrition_plans per active org client (macros only) after deactivating prior active plans.
auditFindings.fragmentation: Org nutrition is split across two unrelated template tables (org_nutrition_templates vs nutrition_plan_templates+coach_id NULL) surfaced on one page with two creation UIs (modal form posting a JSON payload vs a full /nutrition/new page posting FormData), overlapping fields and divergent capabilities (one has meals but is unusable, the other is usable but macro-only). Usage analytics are computed two incompatible ways: a name+macro heuristic for org_nutrition_templates vs an unused real template_id FK on nutrition_plans. Teams add a THIRD nutrition sharing mechanism (nutrition_plan_templates.team_id pool) with no UI, totally disjoint from the org tables. Catalog org_id columns exist but are inert, so foods are still global/standalone-scoped inside org context.
auditFindings.professionalGaps: A registered dietitian managing an org library would find: no actual food/meal content carried into assigned plans (assignment is macros only — calories/P/C/F with no foods, portions, meal timing, or the meal_names the admin defined); no micronutrients, fiber, hydration, or fat-quality breakdown — only the 4 macro integers; goal_type is a free-text label with no kcal/macro derivation or validation (e.g. protein g/kg of bodyweight); no per-client individualization on assignment (one template blasted to everyone regardless of weight, sex, activity, allergies/intolerances despite a free-text 'restricciones' note that is never structured); adherence is logged-vs-prescribed presence only (did they log), not nutrient-target compliance; exchange/intercambio plan_mode exists in the F7 table but is absent from the org template builder, so org templates can't express exchange-based prescriptions an RD would actually use.

auditFindings.summary:
EVA's nutrition backend splits into two generations living side by side. The LEGACY "grams" engine (foods with per-100g/ml macros) is modeled around plan -> meals -> food_items, plus a template propagation system (nutrition_plan_templates -> template_meals -> template_meal_groups -> saved_meals -> saved_meal_items) and a cycle layer (nutrition_plan_cycles with week-range blocks pointing at templates). NutritionService is a thin class wrapping a Supabase client that performs template CRUD, propagation to synced clients (in-place updates to preserve meal_logs), and duplication. Macro math lives PURE in lib/nutrition-utils.ts (calculateFoodItemMacros: g/ml proportional to /100, 'un' uses serving_size), with adherence/consumed calc, swap-option resolution, and a completion-ratio fallback. The NEWER "exchanges" module (nutrition_exchanges) is a properly clean-architecture vertical: pure domain types (exchange.types.ts), a pure calc engine (exchange-calc.ts: Sigma portions x ref macros per group, with composed-group expansion like LEG -> 1P+1C), pure reconciliation (meal-reconcile.ts: match meals by DB id to keep targets/variants attached), a dedicated repository (exchanges.repository.ts), and a service (nutrition-exchanges.service.ts) that does resource-context gating, tenant verification, and best-effort PDF audit logging. Schemas are centralized in packages/schemas (shared with apps/mobile).

The architectural quality is bimodal: the exchanges module is exemplary clean architecture (pure domain/calc, repository boundary, no Next/Supabase leakage, defense-in-depth tenant checks, unit-testable). The legacy grams path violates the stated clean-architecture data flow — NutritionService talks to Supabase directly with table-name string literals and raw inserts/updates instead of going through nutrition.repository.ts, which only exposes a handful of read functions. The two generations are also not unified: there is no single "compute plan macros" surface, foods carry both legacy macro columns and exchange_* columns, and the actual client-plan SAVE path (upsertClientNutritionPlanJson) lives in the actions layer, not the service.

auditFindings.dataFlow:
TABLES (legacy grams): nutrition_plans (client-scoped instance: daily_calories/protein_g/carbs_g/fats_g, template_id, template_version_id, plan_mode, last_edited_by_coach_id, is_active, is_custom) -> nutrition_meals (name, order_index, day_of_week, day_variant_id) -> food_items (food_id, quantity, unit, swap_options jsonb) -> foods (per-100g/ml macros: calories/protein_g/carbs_g/fats_g, serving_size, serving_unit, is_liquid + exchange_group_id/exchange_portion_grams/exchange_portion_label). Adherence: nutrition_meal_logs / daily_nutrition_logs (is_completed, consumed_quantity as pct). recipes table (per-recipe macros, source_api) referenced but barely wired.
TEMPLATE/PROPAGATION tables: nutrition_plan_templates -> template_meals -> template_meal_groups -> saved_meals -> saved_meal_items (food_id, quantity, unit, swap_options). Note the awkward indirection: a template meal points at a 'saved_meal' created internally with name 'Internal_<name>_<Date.now()>' purely as a container — saved_meals is overloaded to be both a coach's reusable meal library AND an internal template join row.
CYCLE tables: nutrition_plan_cycles (start_date, is_active) with blocks jsonb [{week_start, week_end, template_id, label}]; resolver maps today's calendar-week index to a block.
EXCHANGES tables: exchange_groups (system/coach/team scoped: code, ref_calories/protein/carbs/fats per portion, composed_of jsonb, macros_confirmed, color, sort_order, deleted_at soft-delete) -> meal_exchange_targets (meal_id, exchange_group_id, portions numeric, notes; UNIQUE meal+group) ; nutrition_plan_day_variants (plan_id, name, sort_order) referenced by nutrition_meals.day_variant_id (ON DELETE SET NULL). foods.exchange_* gives per-food portion equivalences.
SERVICES touched: NutritionService (direct Supabase, table literals), nutrition-exchanges service (via exchanges.repository), entitlements.service (assertModule/getCoach|TeamEnabledModules for gating), team.service (logTeamClientAccess audit). 
QUERIES: read repo (nutrition.repository) selects explicit columns + uses foods.or(coach null/eq). Exchanges repo batches by .in(mealIds/groupIds) — no N+1 on read. PROPAGATION path is the hot spot: per-client it issues many sequential awaited queries (existing-plan lookup, meals fetch, logged-rows check, per-meal update + per-meal food_items delete+insert) inside a for-of over clients — heavily serial, O(clients x meals) round-trips, no Promise.all, no transaction.
auditFindings.fragmentation: Nutrition logic is scattered across at least four homes with no single source of truth: (1) macro math for grams lives in lib/nutrition-utils.ts, macro math for exchanges lives in services/nutrition-exchanges/exchange-calc.ts — two unrelated calc engines, no shared 'macro totals' type (ExchangeMacroTotals vs the ad-hoc {calories,protein,carbs,fats} object vs domain MacroTarget which uses protein/carbs/fat shorthand and is essentially unused). (2) Writes are split: NutritionService handles template/propagation writes by talking to Supabase directly, while the actual client-plan save (upsertClientNutritionPlanJson) and reconcileMealsById wiring live in app/coach/nutrition-plans/_actions, and a parallel implementation exists in apps/mobile/lib/nutrition-builder.ts (duplicate builder logic across web and mobile). (3) Reads are split between nutrition.repository.ts (a few functions) and snapshot/queries that select nested graphs directly. (4) swap_options normalization is duplicated: nutrition-utils.ts (normalizeSwapOptions / resolveCoachSwapPortionFromSwapOptions / coerceSwapOptionUnit) and nutrition-plan-snapshot.ts has its own pass-through normalizeSwapOptions, and the service inline-shapes swap_options in 5+ places. (5) Units are inconsistent: schema allows g/un/ml but DB migration 'normalized' to drop ml while code still emits ml; is_liquid is sometimes a column, sometimes inferred from serving_unit==='ml'. (6) plan_mode lives on nutrition_plans but the grams and exchanges worlds don't share a plan aggregate — the exchanges save path and grams save path are entirely separate code, with a relaxed schema (ExchangesClientPlanSchema) shadowing ClientPlanSchema.
auditFindings.professionalGaps: For a registered dietitian the model is shallow on several fronts: (1) No micronutrients at all — foods carry only kcal/protein/carbs/fats. No fiber, sugar, saturated/trans/poly/mono fat split, sodium, potassium, cholesterol, or any vitamin/mineral. A real practice can't track sodium for hypertension or fiber for GI goals. (2) No fractional/alcohol macro accounting and no kcal cross-check — daily_calories, protein_g, carbs_g, fats_g are stored independently with zero validation that 4*P + 4*C + 9*F approximates calories; a coach can save internally inconsistent targets (schema only bounds 0..max). (3) Portion model is coarse: 'un' uses a single serving_size per food, no support for household measures per food except in the exchanges equivalence table; no edible-portion vs as-purchased, no cooked/raw yield factors. (4) Exchange macros are 'reference per portion' with a macros_confirmed flag but no link back to underlying food composition — the equivalence list (food->grams) and the group ref macros can silently disagree; no validation that the foods assigned to a group actually average the group's ref macros. (5) No per-meal timing/energy distribution targets, no hydration, no supplement tracking, no allergen/intolerance/diet-tag modeling on foods (category enum is 10 broad buckets). (6) Adherence is binary-or-single-pct per meal (consumed_quantity as one number), so it can't capture which foods were swapped/skipped at the food level for accurate intake. (7) No food data provenance/versioning — editing a food mutates macros for every historical log retroactively (snapshots exist only at the plan level, not the food/log level), so historical adherence numbers drift. (8) Cycle/periodization is template-by-calendar-week only; no goal-phase metadata, no automatic kcal/macro adjustment by body-weight trend.

strengths.title: Exchanges module is textbook clean architecture
strengths.detail: domain/nutrition/exchange.types.ts, exchange-calc.ts and meal-reconcile.ts are fully pure (no Next/Supabase/lib imports), unit-testable, with the repository as the only IO boundary and the service orchestrating gating. composed-group expansion (LEG -> 1P+1C) and day-variant totals are correct and well-commented.

strengths.title: Resource-context gating with defense in depth
strengths.detail: nutrition-exchanges.service enforces module entitlement by RESOURCE context (pool/team overrides coach), verifies every client-controlled exchangeGroupId resolves against visible groups (verifyGroupsVisibleToActor) since the FK doesn't check visibility, and re-checks tenant in pure code (groupMatchesTenant) on top of the SQL filter for the student bundle.

strengths.title: Meal-log preservation is taken seriously
strengths.detail: Propagation updates plans in-place (same plan_id) and matches meals by order_index, and meal-reconcile matches by DB id, specifically to avoid ON DELETE CASCADE wiping nutrition_meal_logs adherence history. The 'only delete meals without logs' guard in propagateTemplateChanges is a thoughtful data-safety measure.

strengths.title: Macro unit math is correct and documented
strengths.detail: calculateFoodItemMacros correctly treats g/ml as direct /100 and 'un' as quantity*serving_size/100, with a regression comment about the old oil bug (15ml*14/100). Rounding is consistent to 1 decimal.

strengths.title: Schemas centralized and shared
strengths.detail: packages/schemas is the single Zod source consumed by web (re-exported via lib/nutrition-schemas.ts) and mobile; z.guid() vs z.uuid() seed-UUID gotcha is handled; bounds are sane.

weaknesses.title: Legacy NutritionService bypasses the repository layer (pillar violation)
weaknesses.detail: CLAUDE.md mandates services -> infrastructure/db/*.repository -> Supabase. NutritionService injects a raw SupabaseClient and issues from('nutrition_plan_templates'/'template_meals'/'saved_meals'/'food_items'...) inserts/updates/deletes directly with table-name string literals. nutrition.repository.ts has zero write functions. The whole template/propagation/duplication write surface lives in the service with no repository boundary, the exact anti-pattern the architecture forbids.

weaknesses.title: Propagation is serial, transaction-less, and partial-failure-prone
weaknesses.detail: propagateTemplateChanges loops clients sequentially, and per client fires many awaited queries (plan lookup, meals fetch, logged-rows check, per-meal update + per-meal food_items delete then insert) with no Promise.all and no DB transaction. For a 300-client team (Movida) with multi-meal templates this is thousands of serial round-trips. Worse, any mid-loop error throws with earlier clients already mutated and later ones untouched — no atomicity, leaving plans in inconsistent states. Many writes also ignore returned errors (no error check on most inserts/updates).

weaknesses.title: Two divergent macro engines with no shared contract
weaknesses.detail: grams totals ({calories,protein,carbs,fats}) and exchange totals (ExchangeMacroTotals {calories,proteinG,carbsG,fatsG}) are computed by separate code with different field names and rounding helpers, and domain MacroTarget (protein/carbs/fat) is a third unused shape. There is no unified 'plan daily totals' function, so a plan's stored daily_calories/protein_g/... are never reconciled against the sum of its meals in either mode. A coach can ship a plan whose meals don't sum to its stated targets.

weaknesses.title: Cycle block ranges are not validated for overlap/gaps/contiguity
weaknesses.detail: nutritionPlanCycleUpsertSchema validates each block's week_end>=week_start but never that blocks don't overlap or leave gaps. resolveNutritionCycleBlockForDate returns the FIRST matching block, so overlapping ranges silently pick one and gaps return block:null (alumno sees no plan that week) with no authoring-time warning.

weaknesses.title: saved_meals overloaded as internal template join container
weaknesses.detail: Template propagation creates throwaway saved_meals rows named 'Internal_<name>_<Date.now()>' purely to bridge template_meals -> saved_meal_items. This conflates the coach's reusable meal library with an internal join artifact, pollutes the saved_meals table, and the timestamped names will accumulate orphans on every template edit/duplicate. A direct template_meal_items table would be cleaner.

weaknesses.title: Pervasive any-casts erase type safety at the IO boundary
weaknesses.detail: NutritionService casts query results and insert payloads with 'as any' throughout (existingClients?.map((c:any)...), template as any, update({...} as any)). This defeats database.types.ts exactly where column-name/typo bugs (the kind that caused the recent coach-dashboard 400s in git history) are most likely. The exchanges repo, by contrast, uses typed Row mappers — the legacy path should match it.

weaknesses.title: Food edits retroactively rewrite historical intake
weaknesses.detail: Macros are read live from foods at calc time (calculateFoodItemMacros pulls foods.calories etc.), and food_items only store quantity/unit + a swap_options snapshot. Editing a food's macros silently changes every past daily/meal log's computed intake. There is no per-log macro snapshot, so adherence analytics are not reproducible — a correctness/compliance concern for a nutrition product.

weaknesses.title: Exchange ref macros and food equivalences can silently disagree
weaknesses.detail: exchange_groups carry ref_* macros per portion (with a macros_confirmed flag), while foods.exchange_portion_grams/label define what one portion IS, but nothing validates that the foods mapped into a group actually average the group's reference macros. macrosConfirmed is a manual badge, not a derived guarantee, so a confirmed group can still be nutritionally wrong.

weaknesses.title: Best-effort PDF audit log can silently drop compliance records
weaknesses.detail: logExchangePdfGenerated swallows all errors in a try/catch and only console.errors. The comment frames it as fail-open by design, but it is positioned as Ley 21.719 evidence for team data access — a swallowed insert means a coach accessed a pool client's plan with no durable record. Acceptable for UX, risky as the sole audit mechanism.

auditFindings.area: Nutrition cross-cutting libs, automation (crons), and PDF/text export
auditFindings.summary:
EVA's nutrition "cross-cutting" layer has three independent concerns. (1) Automation via two Vercel crons: nutrition-reminder (daily 00:00 UTC) sends Web Push to clients with an active plan who have NOT logged any meal today, deleting dead subscriptions on 410/404; nutrition-cycles (daily 11:00 UTC) advances multi-week nutrition "cycle" plans by resolving the current week's block and calling NutritionService.propagateTemplateChanges, idempotent via last_applied_week/last_applied_template_id. (2) Export/sharing: fully client-side jsPDF generation (the plan never leaves the browser) in two flavors — a per-day macro PDF (nutrition-day-pdf.ts) and an exchange/porciones PDF (nutrition-exchange-pdf.ts, with a pure testable buildExchangePdfModel) — both white-label branded via nutrition-pdf-brand.ts (tenant brand resolved server-side, free tier and any fallback collapse to byte-identical EVA branding), plus a plain-text day export for print/WhatsApp. (3) Coach-facing signals: deriveNutritionCoachAlerts produces deterministic adherence alerts from already-fetched props (no new queries), and a localStorage onboarding-dismiss helper.

Data-quality: a standalone service-role audit script (audit-nutrition-empty-meals.mjs) flags active plans whose meals have zero food_items. The last run (2026-04-30) found 17 empty meals across 6 clients of one coach (joaquinamr7), including 2 clients whose ENTIRE plan was empty (carolina valero 5/5, Macarena 4/4) yet still had daily logs — clients logging against a plan with no prescribed food. This is a known, unresolved data-quality problem and the script is not wired into CI or any cron.

strengths.title: Privacy-first, server-free export
strengths.detail: Both PDFs and the plain-text export are generated 100% client-side (dynamic import of jspdf); the nutrition plan never travels to a server. Documented as an explicit SPEC decision (no Chromium, no server round-trip). File names are sanitized (replace non-word chars, 80-char cap) and a legal disclaimer ('No reemplaza valoracion clinica/dietetica/medica') is stamped on every page.

strengths.title: White-label branding done correctly and fail-safe
strengths.detail: nutrition-pdf-brand.ts resolves tenant brand server-side, with free tier and any missing/invalid name/color collapsing to byte-identical EVA branding (poweredByEva returns a hardcoded exact palette). Header background auto-derives a legible dark shade from the brand color via luminance; logo loading (loadBrandLogoDataUrl) never throws and falls back to an initial+color badge. This matches the layout's branding rule and avoids half-branded PDFs.

strengths.title: Cycle automation is idempotent and scope-aware
strengths.detail: runNutritionCyclesAutomation guards re-application via last_applied_week + last_applied_template_id, so re-runs are no-ops. It deliberately reads org_id from the client (not the cycle row) before calling propagateTemplateChanges, with a detailed comment explaining that omitting it would make an ORG student's plan be treated as 'new' and wrongly re-scoped/duplicated. Good defensive design around a known footgun.

strengths.title: Reminder cron is well-behaved
strengths.detail: Bearer CRON_SECRET auth (fail-closed if secret unset), VAPID validated up front, only notifies clients with an active plan who have NOT logged today, and self-heals by deleting subscriptions that return 410 Gone / 404. Returns a structured {sent, failed, skipped} summary and logs a one-line metric.

strengths.title: Coach alerts are deterministic and query-free
strengths.detail: deriveNutritionCoachAlerts is a pure function over already-aggregated tab props (no extra DB load), covering over-restriction (<1200 kcal), adherence drop, monthly stagnation, and 5-day silence. Easy to unit test and reason about; thresholds are explicit.

strengths.title: Exchange PDF model is pure and unit-testable
strengths.detail: buildExchangePdfModel is separated from rendering, computes nomenclature/equivalences/weekly shopping list, and propagates a macrosProvisional flag so unconfirmed exchange-group macros are explicitly labeled 'referencial' rather than presented as exact.

weaknesses.title: Empty-meal data quality is a real, unaddressed production problem
weaknesses.detail: The 2026-04-30 audit found 17 empty meals across 6 of one coach's clients, including 2 clients whose ENTIRE plan has zero food_items yet still accrues daily logs (clients ticking 'done' against meals that prescribe nothing). These plans export to PDF/text and feed adherence math as if valid. Nothing prevents saving an empty meal or activating an empty plan, and the audit is a manual script (not in CI, not a cron, not surfaced to the coach). Macro totals and adherence percentages for these clients are meaningless.

weaknesses.title: Crons have no alerting or durable observability
weaknesses.detail: Both crons only console.log/console.error and return JSON to Vercel. There is no Sentry/PostHog capture, no failure alert, and no record of per-run results. If nutrition-cycles silently skips everyone (e.g. blocks JSON malformed -> parseBlocks returns []) or the reminder push provider degrades, no one is notified. A coach would never know cycle progression stopped advancing their clients' plans.

weaknesses.title: Cycle automation has no per-row error isolation
weaknesses.detail: runNutritionCyclesAutomation awaits propagateTemplateChanges inside a sequential for-loop with no try/catch per cycle. One client's propagation throwing (RLS edge, bad template, scope mismatch) aborts the entire run via the route's outer catch, so every cycle after the failing one is skipped that day and the {applied} count is lost. A bad single row degrades the whole tenant base.

weaknesses.title: Reminder cron has no batching, rate-limiting, or timeout budget
weaknesses.detail: It loads ALL candidates across ALL coaches and sends Web Push sequentially in one request (await per subscription). At scale (Movida 300+ alumnos, multiple coaches) this is a single long-running serverless invocation that can hit Vercel's function timeout, partially completing with no resume/cursor. No Promise.all batching, no chunking, no idempotency marker to avoid double-sends on retry.

weaknesses.title: Reminder relevance is coarse — fires even when nothing is prescribed
weaknesses.detail: The reminder targets anyone with an active plan and no log today, with no check that the plan actually has meals for today (day_of_week scoping) or any food_items at all. Combined with the empty-meal problem, clients on empty/wrong-day plans get nagged to log meals that don't exist. The notification copy is also fully hardcoded Spanish with no i18n and no per-coach customization.

weaknesses.title: PDF macro math duplicated and silently drops items without foods
weaknesses.detail: Per-meal macro accumulation is reimplemented in nutrition-day-pdf.ts, nutrition-day-plain-text.ts, and the exchange PDF rather than going through one shared summing path; drift risk across the three export surfaces. Food items with fi.foods == null are rendered with quantity but contribute 0 to totals and show blank macro columns, so a partially-resolved plan silently under-reports kcal in the export with no warning to the reader.

weaknesses.title: Coach alerts depend on caller-aggregated inputs with thin guarding
weaknesses.detail: deriveNutritionCoachAlerts trusts mealsDone/mealsTotal/weeklyAvgPct shapes assembled elsewhere; the 'stagnation' alert hardcodes the word '~30 dias 30%' style text from monthly but the description string interpolates Math.round(monthly) — minor, but the alert text and thresholds are not unit-tested here and would drift silently if upstream aggregation changes.

weaknesses.title: Two VAPID public-key env vars must be kept in sync manually
weaknesses.detail: The cron reads server-side VAPID_PUBLIC_KEY while the client reads NEXT_PUBLIC_VAPID_PUBLIC_KEY (same value, two vars). If they drift, push encryption/subscription mismatch can cause silent send failures that only surface as 'failed' counts. No runtime assertion that the two match.

auditFindings.dataFlow: Crons (service-role client). nutrition-cycles reads nutrition_plan_cycles (is_active) + clients(org_id) -> NutritionService.propagateTemplateChanges -> writes last_applied_week/template_id back. nutrition-reminder reads push_subscriptions JOIN clients->coaches(slug)->nutrition_plans(is_active) and daily_nutrition_logs(log_date=today), sends Web Push, deletes stale push_subscriptions on 410/404. Audit script reads nutrition_plans, clients, coaches, nutrition_meals, food_items, daily_nutrition_logs->nutrition_meal_logs(is_completed). Exports read in-browser plan/meal/food_items + tenant brand (server-resolved via proxy headers x-coach-brand-name/primary-color/logo-url/subscription-tier). Tables/relations touched: nutrition_plan_cycles, nutrition_plans, nutrition_meals, food_items, daily_nutrition_logs, nutrition_meal_logs, push_subscriptions, clients, coaches.
auditFindings.fragmentation: Per-meal macro summation is implemented three separate times across export surfaces (nutrition-day-pdf.ts, nutrition-day-plain-text.ts, and exchange-calc used by nutrition-exchange-pdf.ts) instead of one shared summer, so totals can drift between PDF, text, and exchange outputs. Branding logic is centralized well (nutrition-pdf-brand.ts) but the two PDF renderers each redefine their own NEUTRAL/C color constant blocks and their own checkPage/hLine/filledRect helpers — near-duplicate jsPDF scaffolding. Observability is fragmented per-cron (each route does its own console logging, no shared cron-instrumentation helper). Data-quality validation lives only in an out-of-band script and its markdown output, disconnected from the app's plan-save path and from CI.
auditFindings.professionalGaps: A registered dietitian would find: (1) No guard against prescribing/activating an empty plan or empty meal — the system already has real clients on fully-empty plans, which is clinically nonsensical and inflates/voids adherence metrics. (2) The PDF exposes only kcal + P/C/F; no micronutrients, no fiber, no per-meal timing guidance beyond a free-text note, and exchange-group macros can be flagged 'referencial' (unvalidated) yet still drive the numbers. (3) Reminders are a blunt 'log your meals' nudge with no link to actual adherence coaching, no escalation, and no respect for day-of-week meal scoping. (4) The under-1200-kcal alert is the only clinical-safety signal; there's no high-end sanity bound, no protein-floor check, and no flag when totals diverge from the stated daily targets. (5) Cycle automation advances templates on a calendar with no checkpoint requiring the nutritionist to review/approve the next block before it auto-applies to the client.

auditFindings.area: Nutrition data model + RLS (Supabase Postgres, EVA prod)
auditFindings.summary:
EVA's nutrition domain is a coach->client prescription + client logging model layered over a 3-way multi-tenant scoping (standalone coach / enterprise org / flat team pool). The prescription side: `nutrition_plans` (one per client, coach-authored, with daily_calories/protein/carbs/fats macros) -> `nutrition_meals` (per plan, with day_of_week + optional day_variant_id) -> meal contents. Plans can derive from `nutrition_plan_templates` (coach/org/team library) and `org_nutrition_templates` (separate org-level template table, partly redundant). The exchanges module (Movida `nutrition_exchanges`) adds `exchange_groups` (8 system groups C/P/F/V/LAC/ARL/SP/G + composed LEG), `meal_exchange_targets` (portions per group per meal), `nutrition_plan_day_variants`, and `foods.exchange_group_id/exchange_portion_*`; a `plan_mode` discriminator ('grams'|'exchanges', default 'grams') on plans+templates keeps legacy byte-identical. The logging side: `daily_nutrition_logs` (one per client+plan+date, snapshots target macros at log time) -> `nutrition_meal_logs` (per meal completion, is_completed + consumed_quantity + satisfaction_score) plus `nutrition_meal_food_swaps` (client substitutes a food on a given day). `foods` is the catalog (343 rows: 316 global, 27 coach-custom, 0 org). `saved_meals`/`saved_meal_items` are coach meal presets. `nutrition_plan_history` and `nutrition_plan_cycles` add versioning/periodization. RLS is enabled on ALL 15 nutrition tables.

Multi-tenant safety is mostly sound (the exchanges migration deliberately avoids the correlated-EXISTS anti-pattern that caused a 2026-06-09 prod incident, using set-returning SECURITY DEFINER helpers via `col IN (SELECT helper())` and `(select auth.uid())` InitPlan wrapping). The main risks are: (1) column-level UPDATE grants on `nutrition_plans` and `foods` still expose scope columns (client_id, coach_id, org_id) to `authenticated`, leaving cross-tenant re-scoping gated only by RLS WITH CHECK rather than DB-level grant revocation (contrary to the clients-scoping hardening pattern in CLAUDE.md); (2) heavy policy duplication on `nutrition_meals` and `nutrition_plans` (legacy + new policies coexist) producing multiple_permissive_policies warnings across all roles.
auditFindings.dataFlow:
ER (prescription -> logging):

nutrition_plans 1--* nutrition_meals (plan_id CASCADE; day_of_week smallint; day_variant_id FK->nutrition_plan_day_variants SET NULL).
nutrition_plans 1--* nutrition_plan_day_variants (plan_id CASCADE).
nutrition_meals 1--* meal_exchange_targets (meal_id CASCADE, exchange_group_id FK->exchange_groups, UNIQUE(meal_id,exchange_group_id), portions numeric CHECK 0<p<=99).

Templates: nutrition_plan_templates (coach_id author + org_id + team_id marker, plan_mode); org_nutrition_templates (org_id, meal_names jsonb) -- a parallel/overlapping template store.
Logging: clients 1--* daily_nutrition_logs (client_id CASCADE, plan_id CASCADE, UNIQUE(client_id,plan_id,log_date); snapshots target_*_at_log).
