strengths.title: RLS enabled on all 15 nutrition tables with non-correlated policy form
strengths.detail: Every nutrition table (nutrition_plans/meals/meal_logs, daily_nutrition_logs, foods, exchange_groups, meal_exchange_targets, day_variants, swaps, templates, history, cycles, saved_meals/items, org_nutrition_templates) has relrowsecurity=true. The exchanges module (20260611093001) deliberately uses set-returning STABLE SECURITY DEFINER helpers as `col IN (SELECT helper())` and wraps auth.uid()/auth.role() in subselects (InitPlan), explicitly avoiding the per-row correlated-EXISTS SECURITY DEFINER pattern that took prod down on 2026-06-09. This is mature, incident-informed RLS design.

strengths.title: Tenant guard for the new team_id marker is RESTRICTIVE
strengths.detail: nutrition_plan_templates gained npt_team_id_guard / npt_team_id_guard_upd as AS RESTRICTIVE policies so a coach cannot forge a foreign team_id (the permissive policies do not inspect team_id). This closes a real cross-team library-leak vector before F7 reads by team_id.

strengths.title: Solid FK + cascade + uniqueness integrity
strengths.detail: All child rows cascade correctly (meals/meal_logs/swaps/day_variants/targets ON DELETE CASCADE from their parents), template/cycle references use SET NULL, and dedup is enforced by UNIQUE constraints: daily_nutrition_logs(client_id,plan_id,log_date), nutrition_meal_logs(daily_log_id,meal_id), meal_exchange_targets(meal_id,exchange_group_id), nutrition_meal_food_swaps(daily_log_id,meal_id,original_food_id), plus partial unique nutrition_plan_cycles one-active-per-client. CHECK constraints guard plan_mode enum and portions range.

strengths.title: Hot-path indexing is well covered
strengths.detail: Composite indexes match the access patterns: idx_daily_nutrition_logs_client_date (client_id,log_date DESC), idx_nutrition_meals_plan_dow (plan_id,day_of_week), idx_nutrition_plans_coach_org / client_org, FK indexes on all exchange/swap/variant columns, and partial indexes (foods_org_id WHERE NOT NULL, nutrition_plan_cycles WHERE is_active). foods_name_search_idx supports unaccent search.

strengths.title: Target-snapshot-at-log preserves audit integrity
strengths.detail: daily_nutrition_logs snapshots plan_name_at_log + target_calories/protein/carbs/fats_at_log, so historical adherence stays correct even after the coach edits the live plan macros. nutrition_plan_history (snapshot jsonb) adds explicit versioning.

weaknesses.title: Scope columns (client_id/coach_id/org_id) are UPDATE-grantable to authenticated on nutrition_plans and foods
weaknesses.detail: information_schema.column_privileges shows `authenticated` holds UPDATE on the FULL column set of nutrition_plans (including client_id, coach_id, org_id) and foods (including coach_id, org_id). CLAUDE.md's hardening doctrine requires scope columns to be REVOKE'd at table level and re-scoping to be service-role-only (as done for `clients`). Here cross-tenant re-scoping of a plan/food is gated ONLY by RLS WITH CHECK, not by missing DB grant — a weaker, single-layer defense. A coach who finds a WITH CHECK gap (e.g., the org/admin branches of nutrition_plans_workspace_manage) could PATCH org_id/client_id. foods has no column allowlist at all (effectively full-table grant), so a coach can also flip coach_id->NULL to promote a private food toward the global pool if any SELECT-visible row is UPDATE-reachable.

weaknesses.title: Triple-overlapping permissive policies on nutrition_meals and nutrition_plans
weaknesses.detail: nutrition_meals carries THREE coexisting policies doing the same job: legacy `Coaches can manage their own nutrition meals` (ALL) + `Clients can see their own nutrition meals` (SELECT), plus newer `nutrition_meals_coach_all`/`nutrition_meals_client_select`, plus a combined `nutrition_meals_access` (ALL, coach OR client) — and the team pool policy. nutrition_plans similarly has both `Clients can see their own nutrition plans` and `nutrition_plans_client` (identical SELECT qual). Postgres OR-evaluates every permissive policy per row on every query, so this is the source of the multiple_permissive_policies advisor (184 nutrition/exchange entries) and wasted CPU on the alumno hot path. It also raises audit risk: redundant policies make it easy to miss which one actually governs access. Recommend the same visibility-harness dedup already applied to nutrition_meal_logs.

weaknesses.title: foods catalog has no provenance/source column and 0 org rows despite org scoping
weaknesses.detail: foods (343 rows: 316 global, 27 coach, 0 org) stores only name/macros/category/brand with macros as INTEGER (calories/protein_g/carbs_g/fats_g) — no `source` (USDA/Edamam/manual), no external id, no per-100g normalization, no fiber/sodium/sugar, no verified flag. Integer macros lose precision for low-macro or fractional-gram foods. The Edamam integration (EDAMAM_APP_* env) implies imported foods but there is no column to track origin or dedupe imports, risking catalog bloat and inconsistent data as coaches add custom rows. org_id scoping exists but is entirely unused (0 rows).

weaknesses.title: Two parallel template stores (nutrition_plan_templates vs org_nutrition_templates)
weaknesses.detail: org_nutrition_templates (org_id, meal_names jsonb, created_by->auth.users) overlaps heavily with nutrition_plan_templates which already supports org_id. Two tables modeling 'a reusable nutrition template' with different shapes and separate RLS policy families is fragmentation that invites drift and double-maintenance; consumers must know which table a template lives in.

weaknesses.title: Unused indexes add write overhead on hot tables
weaknesses.detail: Performance advisor flags 16 unused nutrition indexes including foods_name_search_idx, idx_foods_category, both swap food-id indexes, idx_nutrition_plans_org_id, idx_nutrition_plan_cycles_* and nutrition_meals_day_variant_id_idx. Some are new-feature indexes (exchanges/variants) not yet exercised, but several (swaps, category, name_search) sit on write-heavy or rarely-filtered paths and slow inserts/updates for no read benefit. Re-evaluate after exchanges adoption; drop the clearly-dead ones.

weaknesses.title: meal_exchange_targets / day_variants lack an org-admin path
weaknesses.detail: meal_exchange_targets and nutrition_plan_day_variants RLS expose only coach-owns-plan, client-of-plan, and team-pool branches (plus service_role). There is no is_org_admin_member branch as nutrition_plans/templates have. For enterprise orgs (archived but motor-vivo), an org_admin managing a plan they did not personally author cannot read/write its exchange targets or day variants through PostgREST, creating an inconsistency with the org-admin capabilities on the parent plan. Low impact today (enterprise archived, exchanges = Movida/team), but a latent scoping gap.

auditFindings.area: Nutrition gating / monetization (paid modules, tier capabilities, add-on billing engine)
auditFindings.summary:
EVA monetizes nutrition through TWO distinct, layered mechanisms that are easy to confuse. (1) Base nutrition planning (create per-client meal plans, macros/calories, templates, the food library, the alumno nutrition log) is gated by SUBSCRIPTION TIER via `TierCapabilities.canUseNutrition`: false for free/starter, true for pro/elite (and legacy growth/scale). The gate is read from `coaches.subscription_tier` and enforced server-side in the RSC `app/coach/nutrition-plans/page.tsx`, which short-circuits non-nutrition tiers into a hardcoded "Disponible en Pro" upsell wall (Pro monthly/annual price, feature bullets, CTA to `/coach/subscription?upgrade=pro`, plus an `UpgradeGateTracker` analytics event). (2) On TOP of that, "Nutrición por intercambios" (`nutrition_exchanges`) is a paid ADD-ON module — one of the 4 add-ons (cardio, movement_assessment, body_composition, nutrition_exchanges) — gated by an entitlement toggle (`coaches.enabled_modules` / `teams.enabled_modules` jsonb), NOT by tier. It is enforced server-side by `assertModule`/`hasModule` (entitlements.service) resolved by RESOURCE CONTEXT (pool/team wins over the owning coach; standalone uses the coach's flags), and crucially `canPurchaseAddon` requires `nutrition_exchanges` buyers to ALSO be on a nutrition-capable tier (Pro+) — so the add-on stacks on top of the tier, it does not replace it.

Monetization of add-ons runs through a sophisticated self-service billing engine (`services/billing/addons.service.ts` + `coach_addons` table + MercadoPago): uniform $9.990 CLP/month per module (owner decision, not differentiated), with cycle discounts mirroring tiers (−10% quarterly, −20% annual). Purchase = an immediate prorated one-shot charge for the remaining fraction of the current cycle, then a PUT raising the recurring preapproval composite amount from the next renewal; the `coach_addons` row is materialized by the webhook. The whole self-service surface is fail-closed behind `SELF_SERVICE_ADDONS_ENABLED` (env, default OFF) and gated server-side at the route level. Team/org coaches are EXCLUDED from self-service — their modules are CEO/contract-driven write-through `admin_grant` rows (price 0, never billed). So packaging is: free planning paywalled at Pro tier; the "nutritionist method" (exchanges + branded PDF) sold as a $9.990 add-on requiring Pro+.

auditFindings.dataFlow: Tier gate: coaches.subscription_tier -> getTierCapabilities().canUseNutrition -> RSC short-circuit in nutrition-plans/page.tsx. Add-on entitlement: coaches.enabled_modules / teams.enabled_modules (jsonb, default {}) read by getCoachEnabledModules/getTeamEnabledModules -> hasModule/assertModule. Entitlement source of truth = coach_addons table (live rows) -> trigger D1 sync_coach_enabled_modules recomputes the jsonb on every insert/update/delete. Purchase flow: POST /api/payments/addons -> canPurchaseAddon -> activateAddonForCoach -> MP one-shot prorated checkout -> webhook materializeAddonFromOneShot inserts coach_addons row (first_charged_at set) + PUT updateCheckoutAmount(composite) on the preapproval. Composite amount: getCompositeAmountClp(tier, cycle, billableAddons) consumed by create-preference, the PUT, UI and tests. billing_snapshots records each charge breakdown. CEO override = syncAdminGrants writes admin_grant rows (price 0).
auditFindings.fragmentation: Nutrition monetization is split across two unrelated gating systems with no single resolver: tier-capability (`canUseNutrition`, read straight from subscription_tier in the page RSC) and module-entitlement (`enabled_modules`/coach_addons, read via assertModule). A coach can be Pro (base nutrition unlocked) yet not have the exchanges add-on, and the relationship is only enforced in two places (`canPurchaseAddon` denial `requires_nutrition_tier` and the create-preference starter-block / downgrade-block) rather than centrally. The 4-module catalog copy is duplicated conceptually across THREE files: packages/module-catalog/catalog.ts (label/pitch/surfaces, no price), lib/constants.ts ADDON_CONFIG (label/description/price), and components/coach/ModuleOffNotice.tsx MODULE_COPY (title/description) — three hand-maintained Spanish strings per module that can drift (only catalog<->MODULE_KEYS is test-cross-checked). The base-nutrition upsell wall hardcodes Pro pricing, feature bullets and copy inline in page.tsx rather than sourcing from @eva/tiers. NutritionPreviewModal uses `plan: any` and reads ad-hoc fields, disconnected from the typed domain. Mobile mirrors tier capabilities in apps/mobile/lib/coach-tiers.ts (a second copy, despite @eva/tiers being the 'single source').
auditFindings.professionalGaps: From a registered-dietitian (RD) standpoint the paid 'pro-nutrition' story is thin and trainer-centric, not clinician-grade. (1) The headline paid nutrition feature is `nutrition_exchanges` = portion/exchange lists (the Chilean 'método de intercambios') + a branded PDF. That is a presentation format, not a nutrition-assessment toolkit. There is no energy-requirement engine (no Mifflin/Harris-Benedict/Cunningham BMR, no activity factor, no goal-based kcal target — daily_calories appears to be a free-text field on the plan). (2) No micronutrient tracking, no fiber/sodium/sugar targets, no diet-quality or adequacy checks — only the 4 macros (kcal/P/C/F). (3) Nutrition is siloed from `body_composition` (a SEPARATE $9.990 add-on): the exchange/calorie plan does not consume ISAK/BIA data, so a nutritionist cannot drive targets off lean mass or composition trends without buying and manually cross-referencing a second module. (4) No food-allergy/intolerance/preference modeling, no meal-swap equivalence engine beyond exchange-group portions, no hydration or supplement protocol, no clinical notes/SOAP, no integration with check-in weight trend to auto-flag plateau. (5) No nutrient database depth surfaced (Edamam recipe search exists but composition/labeling rigor is unclear). Net: it sells the *artifact* a nutritionist hands a client (a tidy branded exchange PDF) but not the *reasoning* a nutritionist needs (assessment -> requirement calc -> adequacy -> monitoring). A genuine pro-nutrition tier would bundle requirement calculators + composition-linked targets + adequacy checks, not a single 'exchanges' add-on.

strengths.title: Server-side enforcement is real, not cosmetic
strengths.detail: Both layers gate on the server: the tier gate short-circuits in the RSC before any data fetch, and every exchanges mutation/PDF call routes through assertExchangesModuleForPlan -> assertModule. Comments and the gating test explicitly state enabled_modules from the client is never trusted; the UI only mirrors for show/hide. The add-on PURCHASE route also re-gates behind SELF_SERVICE_ADDONS_ENABLED and canPurchaseAddon, so the endpoint isn't reachable by a crafted API call when the flag is off.

strengths.title: Correct resource-context resolution (pool wins)
strengths.detail: moduleCtxForPlan / hasModule resolve entitlement by the RESOURCE (the alumno's team pool decides for team clients, the owning coach for standalone) instead of the acting coach — this prevents a coach's personal add-on leaking into team clients and vice versa, and is consistently applied across the exchanges service and the alumno-facing hasExchangesModuleForClientContext (fail-closed).

strengths.title: Money-safety hardening on the add-on engine
strengths.detail: The purchase route stacks several P0 guards against double-charging: already-live-module 409, combo-already-billed (preapproval external_reference inspection) 409, upgrade-in-flight lock 409, and a NO_ACTIVE_SUBSCRIPTION block so it never takes money it can't anchor to a recurring preapproval. The composite-amount calculation is centralized in one function and the server always computes amounts (client never sends price).

strengths.title: Coherent stacking rule for the nutrition add-on
strengths.detail: nutrition_exchanges cannot be sold to a non-nutrition tier (canPurchaseAddon -> requires_nutrition_tier; create-preference rejects starter+nutrition_exchanges with 400; downgrade to a non-nutrition tier while holding a live nutrition addon is blocked 409). This keeps the packaging logically consistent: the add-on genuinely extends Pro nutrition rather than being an alternative path to it.

strengths.title: Anti-harassment / SERNAC-aware monetization UX
strengths.detail: ModuleOffNotice is deliberately neutral (no price, no urgency, single CTA to the catalog), and ADDON_PAYMENT_RULES encode informed-consent text (Ley 19.496/SERNAC) with per-cycle variants persisted as terms_version. The upsell is concentrated in two sanctioned surfaces, which is good packaging discipline.

weaknesses.title: Two parallel nutrition gates with no unifying resolver
weaknesses.detail: Base nutrition is gated by tier (canUseNutrition, read inline from subscription_tier in the page RSC), while the exchanges feature is gated by a separate entitlement (enabled_modules/coach_addons via assertModule). There is no single 'canCoachDoNutrition(coach, capability)' service; the cross-dependency between them is hand-enforced in only 2-3 call sites. A new nutrition surface added by a future dev could easily check one gate and forget the other.

weaknesses.title: Catalog copy duplicated across 3 hand-maintained files
weaknesses.detail: Per-module Spanish strings live in packages/module-catalog/catalog.ts (pitch/surfaces), lib/constants.ts ADDON_CONFIG (description/price), and ModuleOffNotice.tsx MODULE_COPY (title/description). Only catalog<->MODULE_KEYS divergence is test-checked; the descriptions can drift silently, producing inconsistent module messaging across Settings, the off-notice wall, and checkout.

weaknesses.title: Pro-nutrition packaging is shallow vs the 'nutritionist' pitch
weaknesses.detail: The catalog pitch for nutrition_exchanges says 'el método de los nutricionistas', but the paid delta over base nutrition is essentially exchange-group portions + a branded PDF. There is no requirement calculator, no micronutrient/adequacy layer, and no link to the body_composition module. The upsell story ('unlock the nutritionist method') over-promises relative to what the module delivers, which undercuts a credible pro-nutrition tier and an RD's willingness to pay $9.990/mo for it.

weaknesses.title: Nutrition and body composition sold as disjoint add-ons that don't interoperate
weaknesses.detail: A nutritionist must buy nutrition_exchanges AND body_composition ($9.990 each) to do composition-driven nutrition, yet the two modules don't share data — exchange targets/calories are not derivable from ISAK/BIA. This is poor bundling: the natural professional workflow (assess composition -> set targets) is split across two paid silos with manual cross-referencing, weakening both the value prop and the upsell.

weaknesses.title: Hardcoded upsell wall in page.tsx duplicates pricing/feature copy
weaknesses.detail: The non-nutrition tier wall in nutrition-plans/page.tsx inlines Pro price formatting, the −20% annual badge, and a 4-item feature list instead of sourcing from @eva/tiers/TIER_CONFIG. If Pro pricing or the nutrition feature set changes, this wall drifts from the source of truth and from the subscription page.

weaknesses.title: NutritionPreviewModal is untyped (plan: any)
weaknesses.detail: The preview modal types its plan prop as `any` and reads ad-hoc fields (daily_calories, protein_g, meals[].foods[]...), bypassing the domain types. Not a gating bug, but it means the monetized nutrition artifact's shape isn't type-enforced, raising drift/regression risk on the surface coaches actually show clients.

auditFindings.area: Mobile nutrition (Expo RN app — coach + alumno)
auditFindings.summary:
The mobile app (apps/mobile, Expo SDK 54 / expo-router v6 / RN 0.81 — note the in-repo AGENTS.md says SDK 54 while the audit brief said 53) ships a genuinely mature, near-1:1 port of EVA's web nutrition for the everyday loop. On the alumno side (app/alumno/(tabs)/nutricion.tsx) the student gets the active plan, a day navigator with 30-day adherence dots, macro rings (consumed vs target via the shared calculateConsumedMacrosWithCompletionFallback engine ported verbatim in lib/nutrition-utils.ts), per-meal completion toggle with optimistic update + haptics, "how much did you eat" portion % (25/50/75/100), 1-3 satisfaction score, coach instructions accordion, a habits tracker, a streak banner, an adherence heatmap strip, and native Share. Offline is real: lib/nutrition-offline-cache.ts persists a read-model in AsyncStorage and lib/offline-cache.ts has a dedicated nutrition toggle queue (enqueueNutritionToggle/flushNutritionQueue) flushed on reconnect and on app-foreground via AppState — a native affordance the web cannot match. The coach side is strong too: a Nutrición hub (templates / clients / foods) with stats, a worst-first adherence board with sparklines, plan CRUD, activate/duplicate/copy-to-client, template assign-to-many with replace warning, a full plan builder (app/coach/nutrition-builder.tsx) with meals, day-of-week scheduling, food items with unit/qty, reorder, per-item swap (intercambio) editor via @gorhom/bottom-sheet, Mifflin-St Jeor macro calculator, auto-totals-from-foods, and a foods library (app/coach/foods.tsx) with mine/catalog scope, search, and custom-food CRUD. A Pro-tier upsell gates the whole coach hub (canUseNutrition).

The maturity is high for the daily loop but the parity is incomplete in two systematic ways: (1) the alumno is read-mostly — it can mark/portion/rate meals but cannot APPLY a coach-defined food swap, even though the query already fetches nutrition_meal_food_swaps and the data is in hand; (2) the entire nutrition-exchanges (intercambios por equivalencias / Movida) module that exists on web — coach ExchangeModePanel/ExchangeTargetsEditor and alumno ExchangeMealChips/ExchangeEquivalencesSheet — is absent on mobile, as are web's OrgTemplatesSection and NutritionOnboarding. And despite expo-camera being a declared dependency, there is zero camera/barcode/photo affordance anywhere in nutrition (no barcode food lookup, no meal photo logging) — the single biggest "native affordance not used."

auditFindings.dataFlow: Reads/writes Supabase (PostgREST) directly from RN — no repository/service layer (web's clean-arch is not mirrored). Tables: nutrition_plans, nutrition_meals, nutrition_meal_food_items, foods, daily_nutrition_logs, nutrition_meal_logs, nutrition_meal_food_swaps (read-only on mobile), plus workout_programs/clients for context and daily habits. Shared pure logic reused via lib/nutrition-utils.ts and lib/nutrition-coach-alerts.ts (ported verbatim from web for parity). Offline read-model + write queue in AsyncStorage. The home widget (NutritionDailySummaryWidget) issues its OWN plan+log query rather than reusing getActiveNutritionPlanFull/getNutritionLogForDate from lib/nutrition.queries.ts — duplicated read path.
auditFindings.fragmentation: Web/mobile parity gaps (mobile-missing): (1) Alumno cannot apply a coach-defined food swap — web has applyMealFoodSwap in nutrition.actions.ts and ExchangeMealChips UI; mobile FETCHES nutrition_meal_food_swaps and FoodItemRow even shows a passive 'swap' badge, but MealCardExpandable never wires an apply action and activeSwapMealIds is never passed in from nutricion.tsx (dead prop). (2) The whole nutrition-exchanges (intercambios por equivalencias / Movida) module is absent on mobile — web coach has ExchangeModePanel + ExchangeTargetsEditor, alumno has ExchangeMealChips + ExchangeEquivalencesSheet. (3) Alumno food preferences/favorites: web has toggleClientFoodPreference so the student can favorite foods; mobile only CONSUMES favoriteIds (read) in the coach builder, the student can't set them. (4) Web coach has OrgTemplatesSection (enterprise/org shared templates) and NutritionOnboarding — neither on mobile. Internal mobile duplication: macro/consumed computation is invoked in three places (nutricion.tsx, NutritionDailySummaryWidget.tsx, NutricionTab.tsx) with the widget running a separate hand-rolled Supabase query instead of the shared lib/nutrition.queries.ts helpers; adherence/streak logic is recomputed inline in nutricion.tsx AND in AdherenceStrip.tsx with slightly different thresholds (streak uses >=0.5, strip 'completed day' uses >=0.5 but the color buckets use 0.8/0.5). Goal source-of-truth is inconsistent: alumno screen uses plan.daily_calories live, while daily_nutrition_logs stores target_*_at_log snapshots that are written but never read back on mobile for historical days (web shows the snapshotted target for past days; mobile shows live plan targets).
auditFindings.professionalGaps: A registered dietitian would find: no barcode/photo food capture (the single most-expected mobile nutrition affordance; expo-camera is installed but unused) — every consumer nutrition app (MyFitnessPal, Cronometer) leads with barcode scan; logging is coach-plan-bound only, so the student cannot log an off-plan/ad-hoc food or a free meal — adherence is binary 'did you eat the prescribed meal' + portion %, not actual intake, so true energy balance can't be assessed; no micronutrients/fiber/sugar/sodium anywhere (only kcal + P/C/F), which blocks clinical work (the coach-alert even warns '<1200 kcal hard to cover micronutrients' yet no micros are tracked); no water/hydration goal target (habits show water_ml as a free number with no target); the alumno cannot apply prescribed exchanges/swaps on mobile, defeating the exchange-list methodology RDs rely on for flexibility/adherence; no portion-size visual guidance or recipe/meal-prep support; satisfaction is a 3-emoji proxy with no hunger/fullness or symptom logging; macros are computed from coach-entered food values with no validated food database (Edamam exists on web per env vars but is not surfaced in mobile food search).

strengths.title: Robust offline-first logging (true native affordance)
strengths.detail: Dedicated nutrition toggle queue in lib/offline-cache.ts (enqueueNutritionToggle/flushNutritionQueue) plus AsyncStorage read-model in nutrition-offline-cache.ts. Flushes on NetInfo reconnect AND on app-foreground via AppState; on a failed online write it falls back to enqueue instead of dropping the change (S1 fix). The web cannot offer this.

strengths.title: Shared pure logic ported 1:1 for parity
strengths.detail: lib/nutrition-utils.ts (macro/swap math, calculateConsumedMacrosWithCompletionFallback) and lib/nutrition-coach-alerts.ts are verbatim ports of the web, so the consumed-macros engine and coach alerts behave identically across platforms — disciplined anti-drift per the app's AGENTS.md.

strengths.title: Coach plan builder is feature-complete for the daily loop
strengths.detail: nutrition-builder.tsx covers meals, day-of-week scheduling, food add/search/create, qty+unit, reorder, per-item swap editor (bottom-sheet), Mifflin-St Jeor calculator, auto-totals-from-foods with manual override, and template/plan save — genuinely on par with the web PlanBuilder minus exchange mode.

strengths.title: Native-feel UX done right
strengths.detail: expo-haptics on meal toggle, @gorhom/bottom-sheet for food search/swap, Moti height animations on expandable meal cards, FlashList for the food library, Share API for plan sharing, safe-area-context throughout, and a worst-first coach adherence board with victory-native sparklines.

strengths.title: Coach client-detail nutrition analytics are rich
strengths.detail: NutricionTab.tsx surfaces derived coach alerts, today's kcal+meals rings, 7d/30d adherence + streak, a 30-day heatmap, a consumed-vs-target composed bar chart, a per-day meal/habit detail navigator, check-in weight context, and favorite foods — strong read-side parity with web.

weaknesses.title: No barcode/photo/camera food logging despite expo-camera installed
weaknesses.detail: expo-camera (~17.0.10) is a declared dependency but is not imported anywhere in nutrition (or, per grep, anywhere meaningful). No barcode scan for foods, no meal photo logging. This is the headline missing native affordance — the one thing users most expect from a mobile nutrition app, and the web genuinely can't do it well. Dead dependency weight + missed differentiation.

weaknesses.title: Alumno cannot apply coach-defined food swaps on mobile
weaknesses.detail: nutricion.tsx queries nutrition_meal_food_swaps and FoodItemRow renders a passive 'swap' badge, but there is no UI to APPLY a swap — MealCardExpandable's activeSwapMealIds prop is never passed from the screen (dead wiring). Web has applyMealFoodSwap in nutrition.actions.ts. Coaches define swaps in the mobile builder that mobile students then cannot use, which is a confusing half-feature.

weaknesses.title: Entire nutrition-exchanges (intercambios por equivalencias / Movida) module absent on mobile
weaknesses.detail: Web ships coach ExchangeModePanel + ExchangeTargetsEditor and alumno ExchangeMealChips + ExchangeEquivalencesSheet (a permanent EVA feature per project memory). Mobile has none of it. Coaches who build exchange-based plans on web have no mobile editing path and their students have no mobile consumption path.

weaknesses.title: No ad-hoc / off-plan food logging — logging is plan-bound only
weaknesses.detail: The student can only mark prescribed meals complete + portion %. There is no way to log a food the coach didn't prescribe, a free meal, or actual intake. Combined with kcal+P/C/F-only (no micros/fiber/sugar/sodium/water-target), this caps the product at adherence tracking rather than nutrition tracking.

weaknesses.title: Historical days show live plan targets, not the snapshotted target_*_at_log
weaknesses.detail: toggleMealCompletion writes target_calories_at_log/protein/etc. into daily_nutrition_logs, but the alumno screen always computes goals from the current plan.daily_calories. Editing the plan retroactively changes the displayed targets/adherence for past days — the snapshot columns are written but never read on mobile (web reads them). Data-integrity/auditability gap.

weaknesses.title: Home widget duplicates the read path instead of reusing query helpers
weaknesses.detail: NutritionDailySummaryWidget.tsx issues its own hand-rolled Supabase select for plan+logs rather than getActiveNutritionPlanFull/getNutritionLogForDate from lib/nutrition.queries.ts. Two queries to render the same data on home + nutrition tab (no React.cache equivalent in RN), and a second place to keep in sync when the schema changes.

weaknesses.title: No direct Supabase repository/service layer — clean-arch not mirrored
weaknesses.detail: Screens and libs talk to PostgREST directly (e.g. workout_programs query inlined in nutricion.tsx load()). The web's mandated _data -> services -> repository flow is flattened on mobile; acceptable for a port but it spreads raw table/column names across UI files (parity/refactor risk when columns change, the column-grant gotcha from CLAUDE.md applies to mobile too).

weaknesses.title: Student food favorites are read-only on mobile
weaknesses.detail: getClientFoodFavorites is consumed in the coach builder to highlight ★, but the student has no toggleClientFoodPreference equivalent (web does). Favorites can only originate elsewhere, so the mobile student can't influence what the coach sees as preferred.

researchFindings.topic: Best consumer nutrition-tracking apps as of June 2026: features, fast logging, and adherence design

keyFindings.title: Two variables govern adherence in 2026: friction and accuracy — improving together for the first time
keyFindings.detail: The dominant 2026 thesis across reviews and research is that retention is not about feature count but about adherence, which is driven by (1) low friction to start logging at the moment of eating and (2) accurate data. AI photo/voice logging finally lowered friction without destroying accuracy enough to matter for behavior change. A reasonable AI estimate beats no data.
keyFindings.source: Healthcare Business Today — 'Nutrition App Retention Was Never About Features. It Was Always About Adherence.'

keyFindings.title: AI photo logging cuts logging time ~90% but is ~82% accurate vs ~94% for manual database entry
keyFindings.detail: AI calorie counters average ~82% accuracy vs ~94% for manual DB entry (mean absolute error typically 15-25% on calorie estimates). For weight-loss users the ~12% accuracy gap is offset by a ~90% reduction in logging time. AI photo logging performs best exactly where manual logging is worst: mixed dishes, restaurant meals, home cooking.

keyFindings.title: The #1 failure mode of photo logging is forgetting to take the photo before eating
keyFindings.detail: A 2024 pilot RCT of an image-recognition dietary app for adolescents found it well-liked and easy to use but it did NOT significantly improve intake — largely because people forgot to photograph the meal before eating. Implication: the capture trigger must be present at the eating moment (reminders, quick-relog, widgets), not just fast once opened.
keyFindings.source: TechCrunch / cited RCT via Cal AI review search

keyFindings.title: SnappyMeal study: multimodal AI logging drove high 21-day engagement, but AI features themselves became new friction sources
keyFindings.detail: Academic longitudinal trial (arxiv Nov 2025) of a multimodal AI food-logging app (voice + photo + computer vision) fostered high engagement/adherence over 21 days and increased users' self-awareness of eating habits. Key nuance: the very AI features meant to ease logging also became new sources of friction (corrections, misrecognition). Design rec: multiple low-friction input modalities + reduce cognitive load at the documentation moment.

keyFindings.title: MacroFactor leads adaptive macro coaching: reverse-calculates true TDEE from logged intake + weight trend
keyFindings.detail: Unlike apps that compute TDEE once at signup from age/height/activity, MacroFactor continuously reverse-engineers actual metabolic rate from logged food + scale weight, converging on true expenditure in ~2-3 weeks and auto-adjusting calorie targets to reality. Fully verified food DB (no garbage user entries), evidence-based protein/macro targets, no ads/gamification. Tradeoffs: no AI photo logging (manual search/barcode only), ~32 nutrients (fewer than Cronometer's 84), no free tier, $11.99/mo or $71.99/yr after 7-day trial.

keyFindings.title: Cronometer remains the micronutrient + data-accuracy leader (84 nutrients, lab-verified DB, deep wearable sync)
keyFindings.detail: Tracks 84+ nutrients (vitamins, minerals, amino acids) from lab-verified sources (USDA, NCCDB); user submissions are staff-reviewed before going live. Syncs with Apple Health, Apple Watch, Garmin Connect, Google Fit, Oura — auto-importing caloric expenditure, sleep stages, resting HR into the diary. Gold ($4.99/mo, cheaper than MFP Premium) adds Custom Charts to correlate nutrients vs biometrics (e.g., magnesium vs sleep). Best for micronutrient gaps, dietary restrictions, accuracy-first users.

keyFindings.title: MyFitnessPal: largest DB (20M+ items) and best integrations, but bloated and protein-light defaults
keyFindings.detail: Largest food database (20M+ items), fast barcode scanner, unmatched integrations (Garmin, Apple Health, Fitbit). Weaknesses in 2026: default 50/30/20 macro split is too low-protein for fat loss/recomp; user-generated DB yields inconsistent values for the same food; ad clutter and tiered paywalls make it feel bloated.

keyFindings.title: Cal AI is the breakout AI-photo newcomer: ~2s photo-to-macros, depth-sensor volume, viral growth, >30% retention
keyFindings.detail: Built by two teenagers, downloaded 1M+ (4.8/5 across 274K App Store ratings as of Feb 2026). Uses the phone depth sensor to estimate food volume, then AI breaks out calories/protein/carbs/fat in ~2s. Apple Health integration auto-adds workouts to the calorie budget. Reported retention >30% (strong for consumer health). Put AI photo logging front-and-center and went viral on TikTok/Instagram. Recurring complaint: accuracy variance on mixed meals.
keyFindings.source: techcrunch.com / askvora.com / apps.apple.com Cal AI review 2026

keyFindings.title: Lose It! is engineered for adherence/simplicity; Yazio owns fasting; Lifesum scores diet-pattern fit (but AI pivot hurt stability)
keyFindings.detail: Lose It! aims to make logging feel manageable for beginners — cheaper and more stable than rivals, built for daily adherence via simplified logging. Yazio is best-in-class for intermittent fasting: polished fasting timer, auto calorie adjustment for eating windows, 10+ guided fasting programs with streak tracking (Pro ~$47.90/yr). Lifesum scores how well meals match a chosen pattern (Mediterranean/keto/high-protein) rather than raw numbers (Premium ~$44.99/yr), but its 2026 AI pivot introduced instability — tracker breaking after updates, fluctuating AI accuracy.

keyFindings.title: Streaks/reminders sustain logging, but excessively low-burden logging can paradoxically reduce remembering to log
keyFindings.detail: A 2021 case study found participants in lower-burden self-monitoring conditions had a HARDER time remembering to use the tool than those with the standard app — fully passive logging removes the cue that builds routine. Best practice: keep logging low-friction but present enough (active confirmation, streaks, reminders) to stay habitual. Streaks and log-reminders remain the dominant engagement mechanisms.

bestPractices.practice: Put the fastest input modality at the moment of eating, and trigger capture BEFORE the meal
bestPractices.why: The top cause of photo-logging failure is forgetting to capture before eating. Widgets, lock-screen shortcuts, smart reminders, and one-tap relog of recent meals put logging in front of the user at the behavior moment with near-zero tax to start.
bestPractices.exampleApp: Cal AI (snap-to-log ~2s, relog from history), Welling (barcode + AI photo combined)

bestPractices.practice: Offer multiple low-friction inputs (photo, voice, barcode, search) — not a single passive channel
bestPractices.why: SnappyMeal and the 2021 burden study both show no single modality wins; passive-only logging removes the cue that builds habit, and AI corrections add friction. Multimodal input fits different contexts (packaged vs restaurant vs home-cooked) and keeps logging present enough to become routine.
bestPractices.exampleApp: SnappyMeal (voice+photo+CV), Welling, Cronometer (search+barcode+sync)

bestPractices.practice: Adapt targets to observed reality (intake + weight trend), don't freeze a signup-time TDEE
bestPractices.why: Static calculator TDEE drifts from a user's true metabolism; adaptive reverse-calculation converges on actual expenditure in 2-3 weeks and keeps targets credible, which sustains adherence because the plan keeps working.
bestPractices.exampleApp: MacroFactor (adaptive TDEE coaching)

bestPractices.practice: Use a verified/curated food database, not raw user-generated entries
bestPractices.why: User-generated DBs (MyFitnessPal's known weakness) give inconsistent values for the same food, eroding trust and accuracy. Lab-verified/staff-reviewed entries keep both macros and micros reliable, which underpins the accuracy half of the adherence equation.
bestPractices.exampleApp: Cronometer (USDA/NCCDB, staff-reviewed), MacroFactor (fully verified DB)

bestPractices.practice: Keep logging low-friction but present — preserve an active confirmation step plus streaks/reminders
bestPractices.why: Fully passive logging paradoxically reduces remembering to log (2021 study). A light confirmation tap plus streaks and reminders keeps the behavior salient enough to form a habit while still being fast.
bestPractices.exampleApp: Lose It! (simple adherence-first logging), Yazio (streaks on fasting)

bestPractices.practice: Visualize progress and nutrient gaps inside the daily summary, and correlate nutrition to biometrics
bestPractices.why: Adherence rises when users see the payoff. Nutrient-gap visualization and charts linking intake to wearable biometrics (sleep, RHR) turn logging into actionable insight rather than data entry.
bestPractices.exampleApp: Cronometer (nutrient targets + Custom Charts vs Apple Health/Garmin/Oura biometrics)

bestPractices.practice: Deep, automatic wearable integration so expenditure/biometrics flow in without manual entry
bestPractices.why: Auto-importing caloric expenditure, sleep, and HR from Apple Health/Garmin/Fitbit/Oura removes a whole class of manual work and makes calorie budgets dynamic — less friction, more trust.
bestPractices.exampleApp: Cronometer, MyFitnessPal, Cal AI (Apple Health auto workout budget)

bestPractices.practice: Set protein-forward default macros for body-composition goals
bestPractices.why: MyFitnessPal's 50/30/20 default is too low-protein for fat loss/recomp; evidence-based protein ranges as the default reduce a common reason serious users churn to MacroFactor.
bestPractices.exampleApp: MacroFactor (evidence-based protein targets)

bestPractices.practice: Match the engagement metaphor to the user's actual strategy (fasting windows, diet pattern)
bestPractices.why: Generic calorie counting underperforms when the user's real lever is fasting or a dietary pattern; aligning streaks/scoring to that lever raises perceived relevance and stickiness.
bestPractices.exampleApp: Yazio (fasting timer + window-aware calories + streaks), Lifesum (diet-pattern match scoring)

notableApps.name: MacroFactor
notableApps.whatTheyDoWell: Reverse-calculates true TDEE from logged intake + weight; auto-adjusts targets to reality in ~2-3 weeks; fully verified DB; evidence-based protein targets; zero ads/gamification. Weak on AI photo logging and free access.

notableApps.name: Cronometer
notableApps.whatTheyDoWell: 84+ lab-verified nutrients, staff-reviewed DB, deep wearable sync (Apple Health, Garmin, Oura, Google Fit), Custom Charts correlating nutrients to biometrics. Best for nutrient gaps and dietary restrictions; cheap Gold ($4.99/mo).

notableApps.name: MyFitnessPal
notableApps.whatTheyDoWell: Largest food DB (20M+), fast barcode, best-in-class integrations. Held back by protein-light defaults, inconsistent user-generated entries, and ad/paywall bloat.

notableApps.name: Cal AI
notableApps.whatTheyDoWell: ~2s photo-to-macros using phone depth sensor for volume; viral growth (1M+ downloads, 4.8/5 across 274K ratings); >30% retention; Apple Health auto workout budget. Accuracy varies on mixed meals.

notableApps.name: Lose It!
notableApps.whatTheyDoWell: Simplified, manageable logging built for daily adherence; stable and cheaper than rivals — strong onboarding for first-time trackers.

notableApps.name: Yazio
notableApps.whatTheyDoWell: Polished fasting timer, automatic calorie adjustment for eating windows, 10+ guided fasting programs with streak tracking. Best when fasting is the primary lever.

notableApps.name: Lifesum
notableApps.whatTheyDoWell: Scores how well meals match a chosen pattern (Mediterranean/keto/high-protein) rather than raw numbers. 2026 AI pivot introduced instability and fluctuating accuracy.

notableApps.name: Welling / PlateLens / Amy Food Journal
notableApps.whatTheyDoWell: Welling combines barcode + AI photo for packaged and unpackaged food in seconds; PlateLens cited as a 2026 accuracy+speed top pick with a real free tier; Amy Food Journal logs a meal in 5-10s via messaging-style AI.

researchFindings.topic: Best professional dietitian/nutritionist (RD) practice software as of June 2026 — feature comparison across Practice Better, Nutrium, Healthie, That Clean Life, NutriAdmin, EatLove, Cronometer Pro, Foodzilla, Evolution Nutrition (plus what RDs require that consumer apps lack)

keyFindings.title: The 2026 market splits into two camps: practice-management/EHR vs. meal-planning specialists — almost no single tool wins both
keyFindings.detail: Eight platforms dominate RD meal planning in 2026: Promealplan, That Clean Life, Nutrium, Practice Better, Foodzilla, NutriAdmin, Healthie, and Evolution Nutrition. Practice Better and Healthie are strong EHR/telehealth/billing platforms but weak at meal planning; That Clean Life, Foodzilla, Promealplan and EatLove are strong at meal planning but lack full EHR/scheduling/insurance billing. The common RD stack is a practice-management tool + a dedicated meal-plan tool (e.g. Practice Better + That Clean Life at ~$129/mo combined).

keyFindings.title: Practice Better = all-in-one EHR for multidisciplinary practices, but meal planning is an add-on, not a core strength
keyFindings.detail: Founded 2014 (Toronto), 50,000+ practitioners across 40+ countries. Core: scheduling, HIPAA-compliant telehealth, AI-assisted charting, payments, programs, branded client portal. HIPAA (US), PIPEDA (Canada), GDPR (EU) compliant with BAA available. Meal-plan creation (recipes/macros/grocery lists) effectively requires a separate That Clean Life subscription. Professional plan ~$69/mo; +$30-60/mo for That Clean Life.

keyFindings.title: Nutrium = clinical meal planning + practice management with deep micronutrient analysis and 1.5M+ foods, but no white-label
keyFindings.detail: Portuguese company (Porto), 90+ countries, 350,000+ registered dietitians. 1.5M+ food database with fast USDA import and multiple measurement units (volume/mass/qty). Full suite: scheduling, telehealth, client chat, dietary assessments with vitamin/mineral breakdowns, progress tracking, invoicing. Limitations: recipe DB relies on community/custom entries (no curated dietitian-verified library), and NO white-label — clients see the Nutrium brand on portal and documents. ~$179/yr (10 clients) to ~$299/yr (unlimited).

keyFindings.title: Healthie = best-in-class for US insurance-billing RDs (superbills, claims, payer integrations), weakest at meal planning
keyFindings.detail: Founded ~2016 (New York), built around HIPAA + insurance billing + clinical dietetics workflows. SOAP-format charting with AI scribe, HIPAA + SOC 2 compliance, BAA on every plan, HSA/FSA acceptance, iOS/Android client app, one-click integrated telehealth. Its insurance-reimbursement infrastructure is described as one of the best on the market for credentialed US RDs. Trade-off: meal planning is explicitly the weakest part of the platform; white-label client app is gated to the Group plan (~$129/mo).

keyFindings.title: That Clean Life = the recipe/meal-plan content leader (8,000+ photographed dietitian recipes, 25+ micronutrients), but no client portal
keyFindings.detail: Canadian, launched 2015. 8,000+ professionally developed recipes with photography, full nutrition breakdowns, 25+ micronutrient analysis, 150+ condition-specific templates (gout, PCOS, low-FODMAP, etc.), 100+ recipe filters, seconds-to-generate plans by calories/macros/diet type. Integrates natively into Practice Better. Key gap: NO client portal — plans are delivered as PDFs via email; white-label only on Plus (~$60/mo).

keyFindings.title: NutriAdmin = strongest CRM/admin automation with multi-regional databases (USDA + AUSNUT + UK McCance & Widdowson's)
keyFindings.detail: All-in-one CRM/admin for nutritionists, dietitians, coaches. 70,000+ food items and 330,000+ recipe ingredients drawn from USDA, AUSNUT (Australia), and UK McCance & Widdowson's — notable multi-regional composition coverage. Customizable client questionnaires/intake forms, EMR, Stripe invoicing, secure messaging, HIPAA compliance. White-label branding ships on every paid tier from ~$39/mo. Limitation: meal planning is functional but basic vs. specialist tools.

keyFindings.title: EatLove Pro = AI-driven hyper-personalized meal plans with disease-state templates, but opaque pricing and US-only focus
keyFindings.detail: AI generates personalized meal plans/recipes/grocery lists from goals, preferences, allergies, and medical data over a 5,000+ photographed recipe library. Out-of-the-box disease-state templates: diabetes, chronic kidney disease, cardiac, IBS, medical weight loss. Client-facing adherence/progress app, Zoom integration. Built primarily for US Registered Dietitians in private practice. Limitation: no public pricing — sales-demo/quote only.

keyFindings.title: Cronometer Pro = the micronutrient/clinical-accuracy gold standard (USDA FoodData Central + NCCDB, 84-92 nutrients), citable in reports
keyFindings.detail: Web platform for dietitians/researchers/hospitals. 800,000+ manually verified foods sourced from USDA FoodData Central and the NCCDB — the same databases used in clinical nutrition research — so RDs can cite USDA values directly in patient reports. Tracks 84 nutrients (up to 92 compounds): all macros, 13 essential vitamins, 17 minerals, individual amino acids, fatty acid subtypes. No user-submitted entries to the main DB. Native Dexcom G7 + Libre 3 CGM integration (April 2026). Pro tier ~$39.99/mo for multi-client management and advanced reporting. Limitation: it's a tracking/analysis tool, not a practice-management or strong meal-plan-building tool.

keyFindings.title: Specialist meal-plan tools (Foodzilla, Evolution Nutrition, Promealplan) lead on white-label branded client delivery and macro auto-scaling
keyFindings.detail: Foodzilla: 800+ dietitian recipes, custom-branded app on Professional (~$35/mo annual), 11-language PDF support. Evolution Nutrition: 700+ RD-built templates (sports/hypertrophy/endurance focus) with macro-targeting that auto-scales portions, white-label on Pro (~$49/mo). Promealplan: 1,000+ dietitian-crafted recipes with verified macros, deterministic macro recalculation, branded client portal even on free tier — but no telehealth/scheduling/EHR.

keyFindings.title: 'Rdlogic' has no notable 2026 footprint; desktop clinical-analysis tools (Food Processor/Trustwell, FoodWorks, Nutritics, Nutritionist Pro, DietMaster) remain the analysis backbone
keyFindings.detail: No credible 2026 product called 'Rdlogic'/'RD Logic' surfaced in market roundups (likely a confusion with generic RD-focused tools). The heavyweight clinical-analysis category is held by desktop/enterprise tools: Trustwell Food Processor (146,000+ items, 55+ report options, label/menu compliance for US/Canada/EU-UK/China), FoodWorks Online, Nutritics, Nutritionist Pro, and DietMaster — used for diet analysis, food labeling, menu creation, and importing data from consumer apps.

bestPractices.practice: Source food-composition data from verified scientific databases (USDA FoodData Central, NCCDB, AUSNUT, UK McCance & Widdowson's) and lock out unverified user submissions
bestPractices.why: RDs must defend nutrient values in a consultation and cite them in clinical reports. Consumer apps (MyFitnessPal-style) crowdsource entries, producing unreliable micronutrient data. Cronometer's no-user-submission policy and NutriAdmin's multi-regional sourcing are why clinicians trust them.
bestPractices.exampleApp: Cronometer Pro (USDA + NCCDB, 84+ nutrients); NutriAdmin (USDA + AUSNUT + UK)

bestPractices.practice: Analyze the full micronutrient panel (13 vitamins, 17 minerals, amino acids, fatty-acid subtypes), not just calories and 3 macros
bestPractices.why: Clinical nutrition care (deficiencies, CKD potassium/phosphorus, prenatal folate/iron) requires micronutrient-level analysis. Consumer trackers stop at macros. That Clean Life (25+), Nutrium (vitamins+minerals) and Cronometer (84) all expose this.
bestPractices.exampleApp: Cronometer Pro; Nutrium; That Clean Life

bestPractices.practice: Auto-scale portions to per-client macro/calorie targets with deterministic recalculation as foods are swapped
bestPractices.why: RDs build individualized plans for 30-80+ active clients; manual macro math doesn't scale and introduces error. Deterministic (not AI-guessed) recalculation gives numbers the RD can stand behind.
bestPractices.exampleApp: Promealplan (deterministic macro calc); Evolution Nutrition (macro auto-scaling templates)

bestPractices.practice: Ship condition/disease-state and exchange/portion templates out of the box (PCOS, low-FODMAP, gout, diabetes, CKD, cardiac, IBS, medical weight loss)
bestPractices.why: Clinical caseloads are condition-driven; pre-validated templates save hours and encode standard-of-care. This is a core RD requirement consumer apps never address.
bestPractices.exampleApp: That Clean Life (150+ templates); EatLove (diabetes/CKD/cardiac/IBS)

bestPractices.practice: Deliver branded (white-label) client deliverables — PDF plans and a client portal/app under the practitioner's brand
bestPractices.why: Plans are professional deliverables; clients seeing a third-party vendor's brand erodes the practice's identity. Lack of white-label is the most-cited weakness of Nutrium. Branded PDF/portal delivery is table stakes for paid client work.
bestPractices.exampleApp: Promealplan (branded even on free tier); Foodzilla; That Clean Life Plus

bestPractices.practice: Bake in healthcare compliance and US insurance billing: HIPAA + SOC 2, a BAA on every plan, SOAP charting, superbills/claims, HSA/FSA
bestPractices.why: US clinical RDs handle PHI and bill insurance. A signed BAA, audited compliance, structured SOAP notes, and payer integrations are legal/operational prerequisites consumer apps cannot satisfy.
bestPractices.exampleApp: Healthie (superbills/claims, BAA every plan, SOC 2); Practice Better (HIPAA/PIPEDA/GDPR + BAA)

bestPractices.practice: Integrate telehealth, scheduling, charting, and payments into one PHI-secured workflow rather than stitching together Calendly/Zoom/EHR/Stripe
bestPractices.why: Fragmented tools leak PHI at the seams and waste admin time. End-to-end encryption across intake -> video -> notes -> billing keeps the whole client journey compliant and reduces subscription sprawl.
bestPractices.exampleApp: Practice Better; Healthie

bestPractices.practice: Provide intake questionnaires, dietary assessments, and longitudinal progress/biometric charting (incl. CGM) the RD can review over time
bestPractices.why: Assessment-driven care and outcome tracking are how RDs justify and adjust interventions; consumer apps track for the individual, not for a clinician monitoring a caseload. Native CGM (Dexcom/Libre) is increasingly expected for metabolic work.
bestPractices.exampleApp: NutriAdmin (questionnaires/intake); Nutrium (assessments+progress); Cronometer Pro (Dexcom G7 + Libre 3, April 2026)

bestPractices.practice: Offer interoperability: import client data from consumer trackers and export structured reports (50+ report types, multi-language PDFs)
bestPractices.why: RDs receive data from clients' consumer apps and must produce defensible analysis and labeling/menu compliance docs. Rich reporting and data import/export are professional-grade needs consumer apps don't expose.
bestPractices.exampleApp: Trustwell Food Processor (55+ reports); FoodWorks (consumer-app import); Foodzilla (11-language PDFs)

notableApps.name: Practice Better
notableApps.whatTheyDoWell: End-to-end HIPAA/PIPEDA/GDPR-compliant practice: scheduling, telehealth, AI charting, payments, branded portal, programs. Best when paired with That Clean Life for meal planning.

notableApps.name: Healthie
notableApps.segment: US clinical EHR with insurance billing
notableApps.whatTheyDoWell: Superbills, claims, payer integrations, HSA/FSA, SOC 2 + HIPAA, BAA on every plan, SOAP charting with AI scribe, iOS/Android client app. Best for credentialed US RDs billing insurance.

notableApps.name: Nutrium
notableApps.whatTheyDoWell: 1.5M+ food DB with USDA import, deep vitamin/mineral analysis, telehealth, assessments, invoicing across 90+ countries. Weakness: no white-label, community-reliant recipes.

notableApps.name: That Clean Life
notableApps.whatTheyDoWell: 8,000+ photographed dietitian recipes, 25+ micronutrients, 150+ condition templates, 100+ filters, native Practice Better integration. Weakness: no client portal (PDF-by-email).

notableApps.name: NutriAdmin
notableApps.whatTheyDoWell: Multi-regional food DBs (USDA/AUSNUT/UK), 330k+ recipe ingredients, customizable questionnaires/intake, EMR, Stripe invoicing, white-label on every paid tier.

notableApps.name: EatLove Pro
notableApps.whatTheyDoWell: AI plans from goals/allergies/medical data over 5,000+ recipes, disease-state templates (diabetes/CKD/cardiac/IBS), client adherence app. Weakness: opaque demo-only pricing.

notableApps.name: Cronometer Pro
notableApps.whatTheyDoWell: USDA FoodData Central + NCCDB, 800k+ verified foods, 84-92 nutrients citable in reports, no user-submitted entries, Dexcom G7 + Libre 3 CGM integration (2026), multi-client Pro tier.

notableApps.name: Foodzilla / Evolution Nutrition / Promealplan
notableApps.whatTheyDoWell: Branded client portals/apps, macro auto-scaling, dietitian-verified recipes, multi-language PDFs (Foodzilla), deterministic macro recalculation (Promealplan), sports/macro templates (Evolution).

notableApps.name: Trustwell Food Processor / FoodWorks / Nutritics / Nutritionist Pro
notableApps.whatTheyDoWell: Deep food-composition analysis (146,000+ items), 55+ report types, food-label and menu-creation compliance across US/Canada/EU-UK/China; consumer-app data import.

researchFindings.topic: Nutrition methodologies and food-composition databases for professional practice (Chile/Latam focus), June 2026

keyFindings.title: USDA FoodData Central is on a fast monthly cadence — v15.1 (May 2026), Foundation Foods refresh (Apr 2026)
keyFindings.detail: FDC shipped versions 14.1–14.4 (Jan–Apr 2026) and v15.1 on 2026-05-21 (new staples: Alaska Pollock, vegetables, breads, pastas, meats, condiments). Branded Foods update monthly in app/API with a full downloadable refresh every 6 months per major release. April 2026 added a 'EM' source = Euromonitor International curated branded-food pilot. Free REST API (api.nal.usda.gov/fdc) needs a free api.data.gov key, ~1,000 req/hr default. This is the de-facto global reference DB but US-centric (US brands/portions); no Chilean products or local preparations.
keyFindings.source: USDA FoodData Central (fdc.nal.usda.gov, Update Log + Future Updates + API Guide)

keyFindings.title: Chile's authoritative food-composition source is INTA/U. de Chile (SAFOODS), not a single modern public API
keyFindings.detail: The Chilean reference is the INTA 'Tabla de composición de los alimentos' (latest print 2018) plus the older U. de Chile 'Tabla de composición química de alimentos chilenos' (Schmidt-Hebbel), hosted via SAFOODS at INTA. MINSAL links composition resources but there is no official free Chilean nutrient REST API. INTA itself flags that Chile lacks sufficiently updated/reliable composition data for natural and processed products. Practical implication for EVA: for Chilean whole foods/preparations, INTA/SAFOODS tables are the gold standard but must be ingested manually (PDF/print) — there is no turnkey Chilean API to call.
keyFindings.source: MINSAL Composición de Alimentos; INTA U. de Chile (inta.uchile.cl, SAFOODS); repositorio.uchile.cl

keyFindings.title: Chilean 'porciones de intercambio' = 7 food groups, built on caloric equivalence + 3 macros + each group's critical nutrient
keyFindings.detail: The Chilean exchange system groups foods into 7 groups (per the food pyramid); each portion is defined by caloric equivalence across the three macronutrients while accounting for the group's critical nutrient. Original exchangeable-portion list published 1999 (~390 foods, raw/cooked, net weight, ages 2+). Current canonical tool: 'Manual de Porciones de Intercambio para Chile', 2nd ed. 2021 (Ratner, Aicardi, Allende, Madrid, Riesco — UDD). The SciELO review (Rev Chil Nutr 2020) notes the 1999 list was long un-updated and the system is used mainly clinically plus in community education/teaching. This is the methodology Chilean nutritionists actually plan diets with — distinct from the US ADA exchange lists.
keyFindings.source: Rev Chil Nutr / SciELO S0717-75182020000300484; Manual UDD 2021 (Ratner et al.)

keyFindings.title: RMR equations: Mifflin-St Jeor best for general/sedentary; Cunningham (LBM-based) best for athletes — Mifflin underestimates in athletes
keyFindings.detail: 2023 systematic review + meta-analysis (Sports Medicine, PMC10687135): in athletes, predicted RMR did NOT differ significantly from measured for Cunningham (1980 & 1991), Harris-Benedict, and De Lorenzo, whereas Mifflin-St Jeor significantly UNDERestimated RMR. Ten-Haaf was most precise (80.2% within ±10%). Cunningham and Katch-McArdle are lean-body-mass-based, so they require an accurate LBM/body-composition input (skinfolds/BIA/DEXA) to be valid. Practical rule for EVA: default Mifflin-St Jeor when only height/weight/age known; switch to Cunningham/Katch-McArdle when a reliable LBM is available (lean/athletic clients).
keyFindings.source: PMC10687135 (Sports Medicine meta-analysis); PMC4183531 (Cunningham confirmation in recreational athletes)

keyFindings.title: Nutrient timing is real but secondary to daily totals; protein distribution > exact peri-workout timing (ISSN)
keyFindings.detail: ISSN position stand on nutrient timing: the timing of protein pre/during/post-exercise is NOT as crucial as regular protein intake across the day — 20–40 g every 3–4 h maximizes muscle protein synthesis. Glycogen is maximized with high-carb intake (8–12 g CHO/kg/day). 'Macro periodization' = matching carbohydrate intake to training load/phase rather than fixed daily macros; the evidence base supports total daily intake and distribution as the primary levers, with timing as a smaller refinement. For an app: enforce daily macro targets first, offer per-meal/peri-workout distribution as an advanced layer.
keyFindings.source: ISSN Position Stand: Nutrient Timing (PMC5596471 / J Int Soc Sports Nutr 14:1)

keyFindings.title: Commercial food APIs: FatSecret is the strongest for Latam/multi-country; Edamam broad but US-recipe-heavy
keyFindings.detail: FatSecret Platform API: 2.3M+ verified food items, 58+ countries, 26 languages, per-country datasets (local brands/supermarkets/restaurants) — pricing is per-market; free 'Premier' tier covers the US dataset (attribution required) for startups/non-profits/students. Its per-country structure makes it the most credible commercial option for Chile/Latam local brands + barcodes. Edamam: ~900K foods + ~2.3M recipes, 40+ diet filters, 200+ health labels; Food DB from $19/mo, Nutrition Analysis API from $299/mo, up to ~$999/mo — strong for NLP text-to-nutrition and recipe analysis but recipe corpus is US/EU-weighted. EVA currently uses Edamam (EDAMAM_APP_ID/KEY) for recipe search.

keyFindings.title: Open Food Facts >4M products (free/open) but Chile/Latam barcode coverage is thin and crowd-sourced
keyFindings.detail: Open Food Facts passed 4M products from 150 countries (2025), free API, GTIN/barcode keyed, has a Chile portal (cl.openfoodfacts.org). But coverage is volunteer-contributed and OFF does not publish per-country completeness; Latam/Chile barcode coverage is materially lower than US/EU, with variable data quality (missing or unverified nutrition panels). Best used as a free first-pass barcode lookup with a paid fallback (FatSecret) and a manual/INTA path for gaps.

keyFindings.title: AI photo calorie/portion estimation in 2026: food ID decent, portion sizing is the bottleneck — and regional/mixed dishes are worst
keyFindings.detail: Food identification ~85–95% top-1 on common foods but drops to ~60–75% on long-tail/regional foods. Portion estimation is the dominant error source: ~15–25% median error on mixed plates with 2D photos, improving to ~8–12% only with depth/LiDAR (Lu et al. 2024). End-to-end calorie MAE typically ~15–25% in controlled settings, worse in the wild (blur/low-light/occlusion). Vendor claims like 'PlateLens ±1.9%' are self-reported marketing, not independent peer review; as of 2026 there is NO peer-reviewed head-to-head of consumer calorie apps. South American grains/dishes are explicitly under-tested in training data — a direct risk for Chilean cuisine. Implication: treat AI photo estimates as assistive drafts a coach/client confirms, never as ground truth.
keyFindings.source: NutrientMetrics 2026 lit review; Fitia/Cal AI/PlateLens 2026 reviews; Lu et al. 2024

keyFindings.title: RDA/DRI remain the population reference; sports nutrition uses higher per-kg targets
keyFindings.detail: RDA/DRI (US National Academies) are the baseline for micronutrients and general population energy/protein, but for training clients EVA's audience uses per-kg sport targets (protein ~1.6–2.2 g/kg for muscle gain/retention; carbs scaled 3–12 g/kg by training load per ISSN). DRIs are population-adequacy thresholds (avoid deficiency), not optimization targets for athletes — the app should separate 'meets DRI' (health floor) from 'meets training goal' (performance target).
keyFindings.source: US National Academies DRI; ISSN position stands

bestPractices.practice: Layer food databases by region: INTA/SAFOODS (Chilean whole foods/preparations) + FatSecret (Latam brands/barcodes) + Open Food Facts (free barcode first-pass) + USDA FDC (generic/reference fallback)
bestPractices.why: No single DB covers Chilean preparations, local packaged brands, and generic foods at once. INTA is the local nutritionist gold standard but has no API; FatSecret's per-country datasets are the best commercial Latam fit; OFF is free but sparse in Chile; USDA is rich but US-centric. A merged, source-tagged catalog with provenance per food gives professional credibility.
bestPractices.exampleApp: Fitia (Latam-first calorie app with strong regional/Chilean food coverage)

bestPractices.practice: Implement the Chilean 7-group 'porciones de intercambio' system natively, not just gram-based macros
bestPractices.why: Chilean nutritionists and the clinical/university curriculum (Manual UDD 2021, MINSAL guides) plan diets in exchange portions, not raw grams. An app that speaks 'intercambios' (with the 7 groups, caloric equivalence, and each group's critical nutrient) matches how EVA's professional users actually prescribe — a real differentiator vs US-built apps using ADA exchange lists.
bestPractices.exampleApp: Nutrium / clinical nutrition software that supports exchange-list meal planning

bestPractices.practice: Default to Mifflin-St Jeor for energy needs, but switch to Cunningham/Katch-McArdle when a reliable lean body mass is captured
bestPractices.why: Mifflin-St Jeor underestimates RMR in athletes/lean clients; LBM-based equations (Cunningham, Katch-McArdle) outperform there but are only valid with an accurate body-composition input. Gating the equation choice on whether the coach entered body-fat/LBM avoids garbage-in estimates.
bestPractices.exampleApp: MacroFactor (adaptive TDEE; lets the data refine the estimate over time)

bestPractices.practice: Enforce daily macro/energy targets as the primary contract; offer nutrient timing and carb periodization as an opt-in advanced layer
bestPractices.why: ISSN evidence puts daily totals and protein distribution (20–40 g every 3–4 h) above precise peri-workout timing. Building timing as the headline feature would over-promise on weak evidence; building it as an advanced layer keeps the app honest and still serves performance clients.
bestPractices.exampleApp: MacroFactor / Carbon Diet Coach (daily-total-first, periodization optional)

bestPractices.practice: Treat AI photo/portion estimation as an assistive draft that the client or coach confirms — never as authoritative logging
bestPractices.why: 2026 evidence shows portion error of 15–25% on mixed plates and degraded accuracy on under-represented South American foods; vendor accuracy claims are not peer-reviewed. A 'confirm/adjust the AI guess' UX captures the speed benefit while keeping the nutritionist accountable and the data defensible.
bestPractices.exampleApp: Cal AI / SnapCalorie (photo-first logging with manual correction step)

bestPractices.practice: Tag every food entry with its source DB and a verification flag, and prefer verified/local entries in search ranking
bestPractices.why: Open Food Facts and crowd-sourced/AI entries vary in quality; INTA and FatSecret-verified entries are trustworthy. Surfacing provenance and ranking verified/local foods first protects coaches from prescribing off bad data and supports a professional/clinical positioning.
bestPractices.exampleApp: Cronometer (known for curated, source-labeled, high-accuracy food data)

notableApps.name: Fitia
notableApps.whatTheyDoWell: Strongest regional (Chilean/Latam) food and barcode coverage; localized portions and brands — the closest competitor benchmark for EVA's market.

notableApps.name: MacroFactor
notableApps.whatTheyDoWell: Adaptive TDEE that learns from logged data instead of trusting a single prediction equation; daily-total-first philosophy aligned with ISSN evidence.

notableApps.name: Cronometer
notableApps.whatTheyDoWell: Curated, source-labeled food database (USDA-anchored) with full micronutrient/DRI tracking — gold standard for data quality and provenance.

notableApps.name: Cal AI / SnapCalorie
notableApps.whatTheyDoWell: Fast photo-to-macros with a manual correction step; good UX model for treating AI estimates as drafts rather than truth.

notableApps.name: Nutrium
notableApps.whatTheyDoWell: Exchange-list and clinical meal planning workflows aimed at practitioners — relevant template for EVA's nutritionist-facing exchange ('intercambios') features.

researchFindings.topic: Nutrition app UI/UX best practices and patterns (as of June 2026): reducing logging friction, macro/calorie visualization, shame-free adherence & retention, weekly review and coach-client loops, meal-plan presentation with swaps/equivalences, accessibility, dark mode, mobile-first logging, and notification timing

keyFindings.title: Multi-modal logging is the #1 friction reducer — and it measurably lifts adherence
keyFindings.detail: The 2026 consensus is to let users 'log the way they actually eat': photo for busy/homemade meals, voice for hands-full (driving, cooking), text for meetings, barcode as the fastest/most accurate path for packaged foods — all without app-switching, ideally combinable within one meal. Nutrola reports its community logs meals ~3x higher than the manual-entry industry average (internal session data), attributing it directly to multi-modal capture removing text entry and menu navigation.
keyFindings.source: Nutrola — Is There an App That Combines Photo, Voice, and Barcode Logging? (nutrola.app/en/blog/is-there-an-app-that-combines-photo-voice-and-barcode-logging)

keyFindings.title: AI photo logging works as an assistant, not an oracle — design a confirm/correct step with confidence levels
keyFindings.detail: Independent 2026 testing: AI calorie counters are ~82% accurate on average vs ~94% for manual database entry. Food identification reaches 85–95% on common foods, but portion/volume estimation from 2D photos stays at 15–25% median error. Best-in-class commercial MAPE ~8.4% (Nutrola); lowest validated per-meal error ~13% (PlateLens). A 2024 PLOS Digital Health study found AI + human review + contextual input is the most accurate. Implication: surface a confidence indicator on each AI estimate and make correction one tap; reconcile all input modes against ONE verified food database rather than trusting the AI guess.
keyFindings.source: Nutrola independent test results 2026; Fitia AI calorie photo accuracy 2026; Nutrient Metrics systematic review 2026; PLOS Digital Health 2024

keyFindings.title: Shame-based UI actively reduces logging — 'adherence-neutral' design increases completeness
keyFindings.detail: MacroFactor's documented philosophy: deliberately NO streak counters, NO red numbers when over target, NO good/bad food labels (no smiley/frowny faces), NO pre-log warning pop-ups. Rationale is both ethical and functional: research shows shame/guilt predict higher body weight, more difficulty losing weight, disordered eating, and exercise avoidance — and when users get negative feedback after exceeding a target, 'it's not uncommon for users to stop logging for the day,' destroying data completeness. Removing shame keeps users logging on imperfect days (social events, celebrations), which yields accurate intake data for the coaching algorithm. This is the single most important retention lever in the literature.
keyFindings.source: MacroFactor — What Do We Mean When We Call MacroFactor 'Adherence Neutral'? (macrofactor.com/adherence-neutral)

keyFindings.title: Logging adherence decays fast; consistency beats perfection, and that's what design must protect
keyFindings.detail: Active food logging is harder to sustain than passive activity tracking: median adherence ~10 weeks vs ~20 weeks. MyFitnessPal usage dropped from 5.4 to 1.4 days/week between weeks 4 and 12. Research links better outcomes to logging 2–3x/day on most days, but explicitly states 'consistency matters more than perfection.' Most-cited acceptability factors: personalization, automation, user-friendly design, and data-sharing with a health professional. Barriers: technical issues, limited food databases, manual entry. Design takeaway: automate capture, never punish gaps, and make resuming after a lapse effortless.
keyFindings.source: PLOS One (Adherent Use of Digital Health Trackers); PubMed scoping review 2013–2024 (PMID 41329042); Fitia 2026

keyFindings.title: Visualization: hybrid is the 2026 norm — ring for calories, bars for the macro split
keyFindings.detail: Progress rings/bars beat raw numbers for at-a-glance goal proximity. The emerging pattern: a circular progress ring for overall calorie/energy progress PLUS linear bars for protein/carbs/fat breakdown, with small/medium/large variants so different users get the detail they want (compact ring on a widget, full dashboard when expanded). Showing total calories alone is considered insufficient — protein/carbs/fat progress must appear alongside. Real-time updates and home-screen widgets (calorie ring + macro bars + water + quick-log shortcut) are now table stakes.
keyFindings.source: Nutrola widgets 2026; Wellness.alibaba macro tracking 2026 guide; MacroFactor dashboard

keyFindings.title: Notification timing: triggered/contextual reminders beat fixed 'optimal hours'
keyFindings.detail: Interruption science: a push triggered by what the user just did or failed to do (e.g., ~30 min after a skipped logging window) almost always outperforms one sent at a predetermined hour. Fixed-time baselines: peak CTR ~8–9 AM and 6–8 PM, lowest ~4–5 PM; one fitness app got a 30% engagement lift by moving notifications to 6 PM. An active JMIR micro-randomized trial (enrolled Nov 2025, started Dec 8 2025, completes Jan 2027, N=200, age ≥45/BMI ≥25) is testing three reminder types at participant-defined habitual mealtimes — loss-framed (forfeiting a reward), consistency/streak-feedback, and neutral — with success = logging within 2 hours of the reminder. Results pending 2027, but the design signal is: reminders at the user's OWN mealtimes, not generic times.
keyFindings.source: JMIR Preprints #100239 / ClinicalTrials NCT07555262; Pushwoosh push best practices 2026; Retail Technology Innovation Hub 2025

keyFindings.title: Coach-client loops: timely + specific + adaptive, consolidated in one review inbox
keyFindings.detail: 2026 coaching platforms (HubFit, Everfit, Practice Better, Virtuagym) converge on three properties: timely feedback ('before dinner is coaching'), specific guidance ('Add 35g protein at lunch' not generic advice), and adaptation to reality (missed workouts, travel). Operationally: schedulable daily/weekly/bi-weekly/monthly check-ins, a single review inbox to triage submissions, side-by-side comparison of progress photos/measurements, and async video feedback (Loom). The pattern is automated data collection so coaches spend time on results, not admin.
keyFindings.source: HubFit; Everfit blog; Practice Better — Best Health Coach Apps 2026

keyFindings.title: Meal-plan presentation: flexible weekly cards + rule-based swaps/equivalences reduce decision fatigue
keyFindings.detail: In 2026 users stick with planners that 'reduce decisions' — weekly structure, repeatable meals, real-world grocery lists. The differentiating feature is swaps: one-tap 'swap this meal' where AI substitutes an alternative that fits the same macros, allergy/dislike-aware auto-swaps, and user-written swap rules (chicken↔tofu, rice↔potatoes, yogurt↔shake). This is the digital evolution of the classic Exchange Lists (ADA/Academy of Nutrition, since 1950): foods grouped by similar carb/protein/fat so any item can be exchanged within a group without breaking the nutrient balance — ideal as the data model behind equivalence cards.
keyFindings.source: PlanEat AI 2026; Eat This Much; Prospre; MTSU Exchange Lists appendix

keyFindings.title: Accessibility & dark mode are mainstream expectations with hard WCAG floors
keyFindings.detail: >80% of users choose dark themes when offered; 93% report less eye strain. But inverting colors fails WCAG — buttons, fields, icons, and focus states must be re-checked. WCAG 2.2 SC 1.4.3: 4.5:1 contrast for normal text, 3:1 for large text (≥24px, or ≥18.66px bold); AAA recommended for both modes. Touch targets ≥44×44pt (iOS) / 48×48dp (Android). Must-haves: text alternatives for macro/food icons, Dynamic Type/text resizing without content loss, screen-reader support, and full parity across light/dark. Note: macro ring colors (protein/carb/fat) must not be the ONLY signal — pair color with labels for color-blind users.
keyFindings.source: AccessibilityChecker dark mode + mobile guide 2026; LevelAccess WCAG for mobile; W3C WAI WCAG 2.2; Apple App Store Connect dark interface criteria

bestPractices.practice: Offer at least 3 capture modes — photo, voice, barcode — plus quick-add/recents/copy-meal, and let them mix within one meal
bestPractices.why: Friction is the dominant churn driver; multi-modal capture correlates with ~3x higher logging rates vs manual entry. Different contexts (driving, cooking, restaurant, packaged snack) each have a low-friction path.
bestPractices.exampleApp: Nutrola

bestPractices.practice: Treat AI photo estimates as drafts: show a per-item confidence indicator and make correction one tap; reconcile every mode against one verified food DB
bestPractices.why: AI is ~82% accurate vs ~94% manual; portion error is 15–25%. AI + human-confirm is the most accurate workflow and also builds user trust instead of silently logging wrong numbers.
bestPractices.exampleApp: MacroFactor / Cal AI / Fitia

bestPractices.practice: Design adherence-neutral: no streaks-that-punish, no red 'over budget' numbers, no good/bad food labels, no pre-log shame pop-ups
bestPractices.why: Shame/guilt predict worse outcomes and cause users to stop logging for the rest of the day — wrecking data completeness and the coaching signal. Neutral framing keeps users logging on imperfect days, which is exactly when data matters.
bestPractices.exampleApp: MacroFactor

bestPractices.practice: If you use streaks, frame them as restartable consistency feedback (e.g., 'logged 5 of last 7 days'), never an all-or-nothing chain that breaks on one miss
bestPractices.why: Consistency beats perfection in the outcomes research; a brittle streak that 'breaks' triggers the same abandonment as shame messaging. Consistency-style feedback is one of three arms in the active JMIR meal-logging trial.
bestPractices.exampleApp: MacroFactor (omits streaks); JMIR NCT07555262 (testing consistency framing)

bestPractices.practice: Visualize with a hybrid: a calorie/energy progress ring plus protein/carbs/fat bars, in small/medium/large (incl. home-screen widget) variants with real-time updates
bestPractices.why: Rings/bars communicate goal proximity at a glance far better than raw numbers; calories-alone is insufficient, and different users want different detail densities.
bestPractices.exampleApp: Nutrola widgets; MacroFactor dashboard

bestPractices.practice: Send reminders at the user's OWN habitual mealtimes and trigger contextually (~30 min after a missed logging window) rather than at a global 'optimal hour'
bestPractices.why: Triggered, behaviorally-relevant pushes outperform fixed-time sends; evening (~6 PM) is the strongest generic fallback for fitness apps (+30% engagement in one case).
bestPractices.exampleApp: JMIR meal-logging trial design; food-delivery push case studies

bestPractices.practice: Build coach feedback as timely + specific + adaptive, surfaced in a single review inbox with side-by-side progress comparison and async video
